content: If the line $3 x+4 y-24=0$ intersects X and Y axes in points A and B respectively then incentre of the triangle OAB where O is origin is
explanation:
For a triangle with side lengths $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$ and c opposite to the vertices $\left(x_1, y_1\right),\left(x_2, y_2\right)$ and $\left(x_3, y_3\right)$ respectively, the incentre is given by

$$ \begin{aligned} & (h, k)=\left(\frac{a x_1+b x_2+c x_3}{a+b+c}, \frac{a y_1+b y_2+c y_3}{a+b+c}\right) \\ & a=A B=\sqrt{8^2+6^2}=10 \text { units } \\ & b=O B=6 \text { units } \\ & c=O A=8 \text { units } \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Incentre

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\left(\frac{10(0)+6(8)+8(0)}{10+6+8}, \frac{10(0)+6(0)+8(6)}{10+6+8}\right) \\ & =\left(\frac{48}{24}, \frac{48}{24}\right) \\ & =(2,2) \end{aligned} $$

content: The equation $x^2-3 x y+2 y^2+3 x-5 y+2=0$ represents a pair of straight lines. If $\theta$ is the angle between them, then the value of $\cos \theta$ is equal to
explanation:
Given equation of pair of lines is $x^2-3 x y+2 y^2+3 x-5 y+2=0$

Comparing with $\mathrm{a} x^2+2 \mathrm{~h} x y+\mathrm{b} y^2+2 \mathrm{~g} x+2 \mathrm{f} y+\mathrm{c}=0$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} a=1, h & =\frac{-3}{2}, b=2 \\ \tan \theta & =\left|\frac{2 \sqrt{h^2-a b}}{a+b}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{2 \sqrt{\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)^2-(1)(2)}}{1+2}\right| \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{rlr} & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=\frac{2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{3} \\ \therefore & \tan \theta =\frac{1}{3} \\ \therefore & \cos \theta =\frac{3}{\sqrt{10}} \end{array} $$

content: The acute angle between the line $4 x-2 y+13=0$ and the line which makes equal intercepts with the co-ordinate axes is
explanation:
Let the equation of the line with equal intercepts be $x+y=\mathrm{a}$.

Slope of this line is $m_1=-1$

Slope of line $4 x-2 y+13=0$ is $m_2=\frac{-4}{-2}=2$

Let $\theta$ be the acute angle.

Then,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \tan \theta=\left|\frac{-1-2}{1+(-1)(2)}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \theta=3 \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1}(3) \end{aligned} $$

content: If the pair of lines $3 x^2-5 x y+\mathrm{p} y^2=0$ and $6 x^2-x y-5 y^2=0$ have one line common, then $\mathrm{p}=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \quad 6 x^2-x y-5 y^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 6 x^2-6 x y+5 x y-5 y^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 6 x(x-y)+5 y(x-y)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-y)(6 x+5 y)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=y \text { or } x=\frac{-5}{6} y \\ & 3 x^2-5 x y+\mathrm{p} y^2=0 \\ & x=\frac{5 y \pm \sqrt{(-5 y)^2-4(3)\left(\mathrm{p} y^2\right)}}{2(3)} \\ & =\frac{5 y \pm \sqrt{25 y^2-12 \mathrm{p} y^2}}{6} \\ & \therefore \quad x=\frac{5 y \pm y \sqrt{25-12 \mathrm{p}}}{6} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

If $x=y$, then

$$ \begin{aligned} & y=\frac{5 y \pm y \sqrt{25-12 p}}{6}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, …[From (i)]\\ & \Rightarrow 1=\frac{5 \pm \sqrt{25-12 p}}{6} \\ & \Rightarrow 1= \pm \sqrt{25-12 p} \\ & \Rightarrow 1=25-12 p \\ & \Rightarrow 12 p=24 \\ & \Rightarrow p=2 \\ & \text { If } x=\frac{-5}{6} y, \text { then } \\ & \frac{-5}{6} y=\frac{5 y \pm y \sqrt{25-12 p}}{6} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, …[From (i)]\\ & \Rightarrow-5=5 \pm \sqrt{25-12 p} \\ & \Rightarrow-10= \pm \sqrt{25-12 p} \\ & \Rightarrow 100=25-12 p \\ & \Rightarrow 12 p=-75 \\ & \Rightarrow p=\frac{-25}{4} \end{aligned} $$

content: The line MN whose equation is $x-y-2=0$ cuts the X -axis at M and co-ordinates of N are $(4,2)$. The line MN is rotated about M through $45^{\circ}$ in anticlockwise direction. The equation of the line MN in the new position is
explanation:
Given equation of line is $x-y-2=0$

Coordinates of $M \equiv(2,0)$

Slope of $\mathrm{MN}=\frac{2-0}{4-2}=\frac{2}{2}=1$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \tan \theta=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=45^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$

When $x-y-2=0$ is rotated through $45^{\circ}$ in the anticlockwise direction, it becomes parallel to the Y -axis.

Since the line passes through $(2,0)$.

$\therefore \quad$ Its equation is $x=2$.

content: The circumcenter of the triangle formed by lines $x y+2 x+2 y+4=0$ and $x+y+2=0$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & x y+2 x+2 y+4=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x(y+2)+2(y+2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x+2)(y+2)=0 \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { Separate equations of lines are } x+2=0 \text { and }\\ &y+2=0 \end{aligned} $$

The vertices of triangle are $\mathrm{A}(-2,-2), \mathrm{B}(0,-2)$, and $C(-2,0)$.

Since ABC is right angled triangle.

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Circumcenter } & \equiv \text { Midpoint of } \mathrm{BC} \\ & \equiv(-1,-1) \end{aligned} $$

content: The slopes of the lines represented by $6 x^2+2 \mathrm{hxy}+y^2=0$ are in the ratio $2: 3$, then $\mathrm{h}=$
explanation:
If the slopes of the lines given by $a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=0$ are in the ratio $m: n$, then $(m+n)^2 a b=4 \mathrm{mnh}^2$.

Here, $\mathrm{a}=6, \mathrm{~h}=\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{b}=1$, $\mathrm{m}: \mathrm{n}=2: 3$

$$ \begin{aligned}\therefore &\,\,\,\, (2+3)^2(6)(1)=4(2)(3) \mathrm{h}^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 150=24 \mathrm{~h}^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~h}^2=\frac{25}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~h}= \pm \frac{5}{2} \end{aligned} $$

content: The straight line passing through $(-3,6)$ and midpoint of the line segment joining the points $(4,-5)$ and ( $-2,9$ ) have inclination
explanation:
Midpoint of the line joining the points $(4,-5)$ and $(-2,9)$ is $\left(\frac{4-2}{2}, \frac{-5+9}{2}\right)$ i.e. $(1,2)$

$\therefore \quad$ Inclination of straight line passing through point $(-3,6)$ and midpoint $(1,2)$ is

$$ \begin{aligned} & m=\frac{2-6}{1+3} \Rightarrow \tan \theta=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{3 \pi}{4} \end{aligned} $$

content: A line passes through $\mathrm{P}(-4,1)$ and meets the co-ordinate axes at points A and B . If P divides the segment AB internally in the ratio $1: 2$, then the equation of the line is
explanation:
Let points A and B meet the co-ordinate axes at $(x, 0)$ and $(0, y)$ respectively.

Then, $\mathrm{P}=\left(\frac{1(0)+2(x)}{1+2}, \frac{1(y)+2(0)}{1+2}\right)$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(-4,1)=\left(\frac{2 x}{3}, \frac{y}{3}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow x=-6, y=3 \\ & \mathrm{~A} \equiv(-6,0), \mathrm{B} \equiv(0,3) \end{aligned} $$

Slope of $A B=\frac{3-0}{0+6}=\frac{1}{2}$

$\therefore$ Required equation is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y-1=\frac{1}{2}(x+4) \\ & \Rightarrow x-2 y+6=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: The joint equation of the bisector of the angle between the lines $2 x^2+11 x y+3 y^2=0$ is
explanation:
Equation of angle bisectors of two lines whose general equation is $a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=0$ is

$$ \frac{x^2-y^2}{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}}=\frac{x y}{\mathrm{~h}} $$

Here, $\mathrm{a}=2, \mathrm{~b}=3, \mathrm{~h}=\frac{11}{2}$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \frac{x^2-y^2}{2-3}=\frac{x y}{\frac{11}{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow 11 x^2-11 y^2=-2 x y \\ & \Rightarrow 11 x^2+2 x y-11 y^2=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: The equation $x^2-3 x y+\lambda y^2+3 x-5 y+2=0$, where $\lambda$ is real number represents pair of lines If $\theta$ is acute angle between the lines, then $\frac{\operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta}{\sqrt{10}}=$
explanation:
Given equation of pair of lines is

$$ \begin{aligned} & x^2-3 x y+\lambda y^2+3 x-5 y+2=0 \\ & \mathrm{a}=1, \mathrm{~b}=\lambda, \mathrm{c}=2, \mathrm{f}=\frac{-5}{2}, \mathrm{~g}=\frac{3}{2}, \mathrm{~h}=\frac{-3}{2} \end{aligned} $$

Since the given equation represents pair of straight lines

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & a b c+2 f g h-a f^2-b g^2-c h^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \lambda+\frac{45}{4}-\frac{25}{4}-\frac{9 \lambda}{4}-\frac{18}{4}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=2 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \tan \theta=\left|\frac{2 \sqrt{h^2-a b}}{a+b}\right| \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ =\left|\frac{2 \sqrt{\frac{9}{4}-2}}{3}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \theta=\frac{1}{3} \\ & \text { Now, } 1+\cot ^2 \theta=\operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta=1+\frac{1}{\tan ^2 \theta} \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =1+\frac{1}{\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^2}=10 \\ & \begin{aligned} \frac{\operatorname{cosec}{ }^2 \theta}{\sqrt{10}} & =\frac{10}{\sqrt{10}}=\sqrt{10} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The joint equation of two lines passing through $(-2,3)$ and parallel to the bisectors of the angle between the co-ordinate axes is
explanation:
The equations of the bisectors of the co-ordinate axes are $x-y=0$ and $x+y=0$.

Slope of $x-y=0$ is 1 .

Slope of $x+y=0$ is -1 .

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of the line passing through $(-2,3)$ and having slope 1 is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y-3=1(x+2) \\ & \Rightarrow x-y+5=0 \end{aligned} $$

Equation of the line passing through $(-2,3)$ and having slope -1 is

$$ \begin{aligned} & y-3=-1(x+2) \\ & \Rightarrow x+y-1=0 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ The joint equation is

$$ \begin{aligned} & (x-y+5)(x+y-1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2-y^2+4 x+6 y-5=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: The distance of the point $(1,2)$ from the line $x+y=0$ measured parallel to the line $3 x-y=2$ is
explanation:
Let the equation of the line L parallel to $3 x-y-2=0$ be $3 x-y+\lambda=0$.

Since it passes through $(1,2)$.

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore & 3(1)-2+\lambda=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=-1 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of the line L is $3 x-y-1=0$.

The point of intersection of $3 x-y-1=0$ and $x+y=0$ is $\left(\frac{1}{4}, \frac{-1}{4}\right)$.

$$ \begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \text { Distance between }(1,2) \text { and }\left(\frac{1}{4}, \frac{-1}{4}\right) \text { is }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \sqrt{\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right)^2+\left(2+\frac{1}{4}\right)^2} \\ = & \sqrt{\frac{9}{16}+\frac{81}{16}} \\ = & \sqrt{\frac{90}{16}}=\frac{3 \sqrt{10}}{4} \text { units } \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The circumradius of the triangle formed by the lines $x y+2 x+2 y+4=0$ and $x+y+2=0$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & x y+2 x+2 y+4=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x(x+2)+2(y+2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x+2)(y+2)=0 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Separate equation of lines are $x+2=0$ and $y+2=0$

The vertices of triangle are $\mathrm{A}(-2,-2), \mathrm{B}(0,-2)$, and $C(-2,0)$

Since ABC is a right angled triangle

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \text { Circumcenter } \equiv \text { Midpoint of } \mathrm{BC} \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \equiv(-1,-1) \\ & \text { Diameter }(\mathrm{BC})=\sqrt{2^2+2^2} \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =2 \sqrt{2} \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Radius }=\sqrt{2} \text { units } \end{aligned} $$

content: From the following options, the nearest line to the origin is ….
explanation:
Distance from origin to the line $\mathrm{a} x+\mathrm{b} y+\mathrm{c}=0$ is given by,

$$ \therefore \quad \text { Distance }=\frac{|c|}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}} $$

Consider option (D).

$\therefore \quad$ Distance of a line $5 x-2 y=3$ from the origin

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{|3|}{\sqrt{5^2+(-2)^2}} \\ & =\frac{3}{\sqrt{25+4}}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{29}} \approx 0.557 \end{aligned} $$

content: If the pair of straight lines $x y-x+y-1=0$ and the line $x+\mathrm{k} y-3=0$ are concurrent, then the value of $k$ is equal to
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & x y-x+y-1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x(y-1)+(y-1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x+1)(y-1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=-1 \text { or } y=1 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Point of intersection is $(-1,1)$

Substituting $x=-1, y=1$, in equation $x+\mathrm{k} y-3=0$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & (-1)+\mathrm{k}(1)-3=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=3+1=4 \end{aligned} $$

content: The line passing through the point $(5,1, a)$ and $(3, \mathrm{~b}, 1)$ crosses the $y \mathrm{z}$-plane at $\left(0, \frac{17}{2}, \frac{-13}{2}\right)$, then the value of $2 a+3 b$ is
explanation:
The equation of the line passing through ( $5,1, \mathrm{a}$ ) and ( $3, \mathrm{~b}, 1$ ) is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{x-5}{3-5}=\frac{y-1}{b-1}=\frac{z-a}{1-a} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x-5}{-2}=\frac{y-1}{b-1}=\frac{z-a}{1-a} \end{aligned} $$

It crosses the $y z$-plane at $\left(0, \frac{17}{2}, \frac{-13}{2}\right)$.

This means that this point lies on the line

$$ \therefore \quad \frac{-5}{-2}=\frac{\frac{17}{2}-1}{b-1}=\frac{-\frac{13}{2}-a}{1-a} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{5}{2}=\frac{15}{2(b-1)} \text { and } \frac{5}{2}=\frac{-13-2 a}{2(1-a)} \\ & \Rightarrow b=4 \text { and } a=6 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 a+3 b=2(6)+3(4) \\ & \quad=24 \end{aligned} $$

content: The distance between the lines represented by $16 x^2+9 y^2+48 x-24 x y-36 y+35=0$ is ......... units
explanation:
$$ 16 x^2-24 x y+9 y^2+48 x-36 y+35=0 $$

Comparing, above equation with,

$$ \begin{aligned} & a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2+2 g x+2 f y+c=0 \text { we get } \\ & a=16, b=9,2 h=-24 h=-12 \\ & 2 g=48 \Rightarrow g=24, c=35 \\ & -24 x y+9 y^2 \end{aligned} $$

Consider,

$$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{h}^2-\mathrm{ab} & =(-12)^2-(16)(9) \\ & =144-144=0 \end{aligned} $$

$\Rightarrow$ Given equation represents two parallel lines

$\therefore \quad$ Distance between two parallel lines

$$ \begin{aligned} & =2 \sqrt{\frac{g^2-a c}{a(a+b)}} \\ & =2 \sqrt{\frac{(24)^2-(16)(35)}{16(16+9)}} \\ & =2 \sqrt{\frac{16}{400}}=\frac{2}{5} \end{aligned} $$

content: The points $(1,3)$ and $(5,1)$ are two opposite vertices of a rectangle. The other two vertices are lie on the line $y=2 x+\mathrm{c}$ where c is the constant, then co-ordinates of other two vertices are
explanation:

Mid point of diagonal $\mathrm{AC}=\left(\frac{1+5}{2}, \frac{3+1}{2}\right)=(3,2)$

Let Co -ordinates of B be $(x, 2 x-4)$

Slope of $\mathrm{AB} \times$ slope of $\mathrm{BC}=-1$

Co-ordinates of two vertices $(4,4)$ and $(2,0)$

content: The joint equation of the bisectors of the angles between the lines $x=5$ and $y=3$ is
explanation:
The equations of bisectors are,

$$ \begin{aligned} & y-3=(1)(x-5) \text { and } y-3=(-1)(x-5) \\ & \Rightarrow x-y-2=0 \text { and } x+y-8=0 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ The joint equation of the bisectors is

$$ \begin{aligned} & (x-y-2)(x+y-8)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2-y^2-10 x+6 y+16=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: If the line $2 x+y=\mathrm{k}$ passes though the point which divides the line segment joining the points $(1,1)$ and $(2,4)$ internally in the ratio $3: 2$ then, $(k+1):(k-1)=$
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{A}=(1,1)$ and $\mathrm{B}=(2,4)$

$$ \mathrm{m}: \mathrm{n}=3: 2 $$

By using section formula,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P}(x, y)=\left(\frac{\mathrm{m} x_2+\mathrm{n} x_1}{\mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{n}}, \frac{\mathrm{~m} y_2+\mathrm{n} y_1}{\mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{n}}\right) \\ \therefore & \mathrm{P}(x, y)=\left(\frac{3 \times 2+2 \times 1}{3+2}, \frac{3 \times 4+2 \times 1}{3+2}\right) \\ \therefore & \mathrm{P}(x, y)=\left(\frac{8}{5}, \frac{14}{5}\right) \end{aligned} $$

Point P lies on the line $2 x+y=\mathrm{k}$.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2 \times \frac{8}{5}+\frac{14}{5}=k \\ & \Rightarrow k=\frac{30}{5} \\ & \Rightarrow k=6 \\ \therefore & \frac{k+1}{k-1}=\frac{6+1}{6-1}=\frac{7}{5} \end{array} $$

content: If the equation of the median through vertex $\mathrm{A}(3, \mathrm{k})$ of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ with vertices $\mathrm{B}(2,1)$ and $\mathrm{C}(-4,5)$ is $x+4 y=\mathrm{p}$, then $\mathrm{k}=$ where p and k are constants
explanation:
Let $M$ be the midpoint of $B C$.

Then, AM is the median through A .

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{M}=\left(\frac{x_1+x_2}{2}, \frac{y_1+y_2}{2},\right. & =\left(\frac{2+(-4)}{2}, \frac{1+5}{2}\right) \\ & =\left(\frac{-2}{2}, \frac{6}{2}\right) \\ & =(-1,3) \end{aligned} $$

Since the line $x+4 y=\mathrm{p}$ passes through the midpoint $\mathrm{M}(-1,3)$, we get

$$ (-1)+4(3)=p \Rightarrow p=11 $$

Also, the line $x+4 y=\mathrm{p}$ passes through the point $A(3, k)$.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 3+4 k=11 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 k=8 \\ & \Rightarrow k=2 \end{array} $$

content: If the equation $\mathrm{k} x y+10 x+8 y+16=0$ represents a pair of lines, then

options.content: the value of k does not exist

explanation:
Given equation is $\mathrm{k} x y+10 x+8 y+16=0$

Comparing with

$$ \begin{aligned} & a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2+2 g x+2 f y+c=0, \text { we get } \\ & a=0, h=\frac{k}{2}, b=0, g=5, f=4, c=16 \end{aligned} $$

Since the given equation represents a pair of lines,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{abc}+2 \mathrm{fgh}-\mathrm{af}^2-\mathrm{bg}^2-\mathrm{ch}^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 0+2(4)(5)\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}}{2}\right)-0-0-16\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}}{2}\right)^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 20 \mathrm{k}-4 \mathrm{k}^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}(20-4 \mathrm{k})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=0 \text { or } \mathrm{k}=5 \end{aligned} $$

If $\mathrm{k}=0$, then the equation becomes $10 x+8 y+16=0$, which does not represent a pair of lines.

$$ \therefore \quad \mathrm{k}=5 $$

content: The acute angle between the diagonals of a parallelogram whose vertices are $\mathrm{A}(2,-1)$, $B(0,2), C(2,3)$ and $D(4,0)$ is
explanation:
Here,

Slope of $1^{\text {st }}$ diagonal $\left(m_1\right)=\frac{3+1}{2-2}=\infty$

Slope of $2^{\text {nd }}$ diagonal $\left(m_2\right)=\frac{0-2}{4-0}=-\frac{1}{2}$

$$ \begin{aligned} \tan \theta & =\left|\frac{m_2-m_1}{1+m_1 m_2}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{\frac{m_2}{m_1}-1}{\frac{1}{m_1}+m_2}\right|=\left|\frac{1}{\frac{1}{2}}\right| \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \tan \theta=2 \\ \therefore & \theta=\tan ^{-1} 2 \end{array} $$

content: The distance between the lines represented by the equation $4 x^2+4 x y+y^2-6 x-3 y-4=0$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & 4 x^2+4 x y+y^2-6 x-3 y-4=0 \\ \therefore \quad & (2 x+y)^2-3(2 x+y)-4=0 \\ \therefore \quad & (2 x+y-4)(2 x+y+1)=0 \end{array}\\ &\therefore \quad \text { The separate equations of lines are }\\ &\begin{aligned} & 2 x+y-4=0 \\ & \text { and } 2 x+y+1=0 \end{aligned}\\ &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Distance between the lines } & =\frac{1+4}{\sqrt{4+1}} \\ & =\frac{5}{\sqrt{5}}=\sqrt{5} \text { units } \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: A straight line through the origin $O$ meets the line $3 y=10-4 x$ and $8 x+6 y+5=0$ at the points $A$ and B respectively. Then O divides the segment $A B$ in the ratio
explanation:
Let’s first write the equations of the two lines like this:

$l_1: 4x + 3y = 10$

$l_2: 8x + 6y = -5$

Both lines have the same slope, which is $-\frac{4}{3}$. This means the lines are parallel.

The shortest distance from the origin (O) to a line $ax + by + c = 0$ can be found with the formula:

$$ d = \left| \frac{c}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}} \right| $$

Let’s find the distance from the origin to the first line ($l_1$):

$$ \text{Distance} = \left| \frac{-10}{\sqrt{4^2 + 3^2}} \right| = \left| \frac{-10}{5} \right| = 2 $$

Now, let’s find the distance from the origin to the second line ($l_2$):

$$ \text{Distance} = \left| \frac{5}{\sqrt{8^2 + 6^2}} \right| = \left| \frac{5}{10} \right| = \frac{1}{2} $$

Since the straight line goes through the origin and meets the two parallel lines at points $A$ and $B$, the origin ($O$) is between those two points. $O$ divides the line segment $AB$ in the same ratio as these distances.

So, the ratio is $AO : OB = 2 : \frac{1}{2}$.

This is the same as $4 : 1$ when you multiply both sides by 2.

content: The perpendicular distance between the lines given by $(x-2 y+1)^2+\mathrm{k}(x-2 y+1)=0$ is $\sqrt{5}$, then $\mathrm{k}=$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & (x-2 y+1)^2+\mathrm{k}(x-2 y+1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-2 y+1)(x-2 y+1+\mathrm{k})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x-2 y+1=0 \text { or } x-2 y+(1+\mathrm{k})=0 \end{aligned} $$

So the two lines are,

$$ x-2 y+1=0 \text { and } x-2 y+(1+\mathrm{k})=0 $$

For two parallel lines $\mathrm{A} x+\mathrm{B} y+\mathrm{C}_1=0$ and $\mathrm{A} x+\mathrm{B} y+\mathrm{C}_2=0$,

perpendicular distance between lines

$$ =\frac{\left|C_1-C_2\right|}{\sqrt{A^2+B^2}} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Perpendicular distance between lines

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{|1-(1+\mathrm{k})|}{\sqrt{1^2+(-2)^2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{5}=\frac{|-\mathrm{k}|}{\sqrt{5}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=5 \end{aligned} $$

content: The point of intersection of the diagonals of the rectangle whose sides are contained in the lines $x=8, x=10, y=11$ and $y=12$ is
explanation:
Equation of diagonal AC

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{y-y_1}{y_1-y_2}=\frac{x-x_1}{x_1-x_2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{y-11}{11-12}=\frac{x-8}{8-10} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{y-11}{-1}=\frac{x-8}{-2} \\ & \Rightarrow x-2 y+14=0\quad\text{...... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Equation of diagonal BD is

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{y-y_1}{y_1-y_2}=\frac{x-x_1}{x_1-x_2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{y-11}{11-12}=\frac{x-10}{10-8} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{y-11}{-1}=\frac{x-10}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow x+2 y-32=0\quad\text{..... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Solving equations (i) and (ii) we get

$$x=9, y=\frac{23}{2}$$

content: If $m_1$ and $m_2$ are the slopes of the lines represented by $a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=0$ satisfying the condition $16 \mathrm{~h}^2=25 \mathrm{ab}$, then ............ .
explanation:
If the slopes of lines $a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=0$ are in the ratio $m_1: m_2$ then

$$\left(\mathrm{m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2\right)^2 \mathrm{ab}=4 \mathrm{~m}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2 \mathrm{~h}^2\quad\text{...... (i)}$$

Comparing (i) with $16 \mathrm{~h}^2=25 \mathrm{ab}$, we get

$$\left(m_1+m_2\right)^2=25 \text { and } m_1 m_2=4$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow m_1+m_2= \pm 5 \quad\text{...... (ii)}\\ & \text { and } m_1 m_2=4\quad\text{...... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Solving (ii) and (iii), we get

$\mathrm{m}_1=4 \quad$ or $\quad \mathrm{m}_1=-4\quad$ or $\quad \mathrm{m}_2=1$ or $\quad \mathrm{m}_2=-1$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{m}_1=4 \mathrm{~m}_2$$

content: The co-ordinates of the foot of perpendicular, drawn from the point $(-2,3)$ on the line $3 x-y-1=0$ are
explanation:
Let $(h, k)$ be the required point on the line $3 x-y-1=0$.

$\therefore \quad 3 \mathrm{~h}-\mathrm{k}-1=0\quad\text{.... (i)}$

Slope of $3 x-y-1=0$ is 3.

Line passing through $(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$ and $(-2,3)$ is perpendicular to $3 x-y-1=0$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{k}-3}{\mathrm{~h}+2} \times 3=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~h}+3 \mathrm{k}=7\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Solving (i) and (ii), we get $\mathrm{h}=1$ and $\mathrm{k}=2$

content: If $P_1$ and $P_2$ are perpendicular distances (in units) from point $(2,-1)$ to the pair of lines $2 x^2-5 x y+2 y^2=0$, then the value of $\mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{P}_2$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { Given equation of pair of lines is } \\ & 2 x^2-5 x y+2 y^2=0 \\ \therefore \quad & 2 x^2-4 x y-x y+2 y^2=0 \\ \therefore \quad & 2 x(x-2 y)-y(x-2 y)=0 \\ \therefore \quad & (2 x-y)(x-2 y)=0 \end{array}$$

$\therefore \quad$ separate equations of the lines are $$2 x-y=0 \text { and } x-2 y=0$$

$\therefore \quad$ Perpendicular distances of the above lines from $(2,-1)$ are

$$\begin{aligned} & P_1=\left|\frac{2(2)-(-1)}{\sqrt{(2)^2+(-1)^2}}\right|=\left|\frac{5}{\sqrt{5}}\right| \text { and } \\ & P_2=\left|\frac{2-2(-1)}{\sqrt{(1)^2+(-2)^2}}\right|=\left|\frac{4}{\sqrt{5}}\right| \\ \therefore & P_1 P_2=\frac{5}{\sqrt{5}} \times \frac{4}{\sqrt{5}}=4 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\frac{x^2}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{2 x y}{\mathrm{~h}}+\frac{y^2}{\mathrm{~b}}=0$ represents a pair of straight lines and slope of one of the lines is twice that of the other, then $a b: h^2$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{m}_1: \mathrm{m}_2=1: 2$

If the slopes of the lines given by $a x^2+2 \mathrm{~h} x y+b y^2=0$ are in the ratio m:n, then $(m+n)^2 a b=4 mnh^2$

$\therefore \quad(1+2)^2\left(\frac{1}{a}\right)\left(\frac{1}{b}\right)=4(1)(2)\left(\frac{1}{h}\right)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \frac{a b}{h^2}=\frac{9}{8}$

content: Suppose that the points $(h, k),(1,2)$ and $(-3,4)$ lie on the line $l_1$. If a line $l_2$ passing through the points $(h, k)$ and $(4,3)$ is perpendicular to $l_1$, then $\left(\frac{k}{h}\right)$ equals
explanation:
Slope of line $l_1=\frac{4-2}{-3-1}=-\frac{1}{2}$

Equation of $l_1$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-4=-\frac{1}{2}(x+3) \\ & \Rightarrow x+2 y=5\quad\text{..... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Since $l_1 \perp l_2$

$\therefore \quad$ Slope of $l_2=2$

Equation of $l_2$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-3=2(x-4) \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x-y=5\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Solving (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & x=3, y=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~h}=3, \mathrm{k}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{~h}}=\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: If one of the lines represented by $a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=0$ is perpendicular to $\mathrm{m} x+\mathrm{n} y=18$, then
explanation:
Given equation of pair of lines is

$a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=0$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}+2 \mathrm{~h}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+\mathrm{b}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}+2 \mathrm{hk}+\mathrm{bk}^2=0\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Now, slope of line $m x+n y=18$ is $\frac{-m}{n}$

$\therefore \quad$ Slope of the line perpendicular to $\mathrm{m} x+\mathrm{n} y=18$ is $\mathrm{k}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{m}}$

Substituting the value of $k$ in (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & a+2 h\left(\frac{n}{m}\right)+b\left(\frac{n}{m}\right)^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow a m^2+2 h m n+b n^2=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: If two sides of a square are $4 x+3 y-20=0$ and $4 x+3 y+15=0$, then the area of the square is
explanation:
Given the equations of the lines $4x + 3y - 20 = 0$ and $4x + 3y + 15 = 0$, we start by identifying their slopes.

The slope of the line $4x + 3y - 20 = 0$ is $-\frac{4}{3}$.

Similarly, the slope of the line $4x + 3y + 15 = 0$ is also $-\frac{4}{3}$.

Since both lines have the same slope, they are parallel.

The distance between two parallel lines given by the equations $ax + by + c_1 = 0$ and $ax + by + c_2 = 0$ is calculated using the formula:

$ \text{Distance} = \left|\frac{c_1 - c_2}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}\right| $

Substituting the values from our equations, we have:

$ \begin{aligned} \text{Distance} &= \left|\frac{-20 - 15}{\sqrt{4^2 + 3^2}}\right| \\\\ &= \left|\frac{-35}{\sqrt{16 + 9}}\right| \\\\ &= \left|\frac{-35}{5}\right| \\\\ &= 7 \text{ units} \end{aligned} $

Therefore, since the distance between the parallel sides of the square is 7 units, the side length of the square is 7 units.

The area of the square is calculated as the square of the side length:

$ \text{Area of the square} = 7^2 = 49 \text{ sq. units} $

content: The value of $k$, if the slope of one of the lines given by $4 x^2+k x y+y^2=0$ is four times that of the other, is given by
explanation:
Given equation of pair of lines is

$$\begin{aligned} & 4 x^2+k x y+y^2=0 \\ \therefore \quad & a=4, h=\frac{k}{2}, b=1 \end{aligned}$$

According to the given condition,

$$\begin{aligned} & m_1=4 m_2 \\ & m_1+m_2=-k \\ & \Rightarrow 4 m_2+m_2=-k \\ & \Rightarrow 5 m_2=-k \\ & \Rightarrow m_2=\frac{k}{5} \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & m_1 m_2=4 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(4 m_2\right) m_2=4 \\ & \Rightarrow m_2^2=1 \\ & \Rightarrow m_2= \pm 1 \\ & \text { From (i)}, k= \pm 5 \end{aligned}$$

content: The joint equation of pair of lines through the origin, each of which makes an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with Y -axis, is
explanation:
Let OA and OB be the required lines.

$\therefore \quad$ angles made by OA and OB with X -axis are $60^{\circ}$ and $120^{\circ}$ respectively.

$\therefore \quad$ Their equations are $y=\sqrt{3} x$ and $y=-\sqrt{3} x$

i.e., $\sqrt{3} x-y=0$ and $\sqrt{3} x+y=0$

$\therefore \quad$ The joint equation of the lines is

$$(\sqrt{3} x-y)(\sqrt{3} x+y)=0 \Rightarrow 3 x^2-y^2=0$$

content: A straight line L through the point $(3,-2)$ is inclined at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ to the line $\sqrt{3} x+y=1$. If L also intersects the X -axis, then the equation of $L$ is
explanation:
The equation of a straight line passing through $(3,-2)$ is

$$y+2=\mathrm{m}(x-3)\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

The slope of the line $\sqrt{3} x+y=1$ is $-\sqrt{3}$

So, $\tan 60^{\circ}= \pm \frac{\mathrm{m}-(-\sqrt{3})}{1+\mathrm{m}(-\sqrt{3})} \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}= \pm \frac{\mathrm{m}+\sqrt{3}}{1-\sqrt{3} m}$

On solving, we get

$$\mathrm{m}=0 \text { or } \sqrt{3}$$

Putting the values of m in (i), the required equation of lines are $y+2=0$ and $$y-\sqrt{3} x+2+3 \sqrt{3}=0$$

content: The joint equation of two lines through the origin, each making an angle with measure of $30^{\circ}$ with the positive Y -axis, is
explanation:
We are given that the two lines pass through the origin and each makes an angle of $$30^\circ$$ with the positive y-axis. To find the joint equation, follow these steps:

Determine the angle the lines make with the positive x-axis.

Since the positive y-axis is $$90^\circ$$ from the positive x-axis, if a line makes an angle of $$30^\circ$$ with the y-axis, then its angle with the x-axis is either:

$$90^\circ - 30^\circ = 60^\circ \quad \text{or} \quad 90^\circ + 30^\circ = 120^\circ.$$

Find the slopes of the lines.

For an angle of $$60^\circ$$ with the x-axis, the slope is:

$$m_1 = \tan(60^\circ) = \sqrt{3}.$$

For an angle of $$120^\circ$$ with the x-axis, the slope is:

$$m_2 = \tan(120^\circ) = \tan(180^\circ - 60^\circ) = -\tan(60^\circ) = -\sqrt{3}.$$

Write the equations of the lines.

Since both lines pass through the origin, their equations are:

$$y = \sqrt{3}\,x \quad \text{and} \quad y = -\sqrt{3}\,x.$$

Form the joint equation.

The combined equation is obtained by setting the product of the factors equal to zero:

$$\Bigl(y - \sqrt{3}\,x\Bigr)\Bigl(y + \sqrt{3}\,x\Bigr) = 0.$$

Expanding this:

$$y^2 - (\sqrt{3}\,x)^2 = y^2 - 3x^2 = 0.$$

Multiplying the entire equation by $$-1$$ (which does not change the solutions), we get:

$$3x^2 - y^2 = 0.$$

Compare with the given options.

Option C states: $$3x^2 - y^2 = 0,$$ which matches our result.

Thus, the correct answer is Option C.

content: If the slope of one of the lines given by $\mathrm{K} x^2+6 x y+y^2=0$ is three times the order, then the value of $K$ is
explanation:
Given equation of pair of lines is

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{K} x^2+6 x y+y^2=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{~A}=\mathrm{K}, \mathrm{H}=3, \mathrm{~B}=1 \end{aligned}$$

Let the slopes of the lines be $m_1$ and $m_2$

$\mathrm{m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2=\frac{-2 \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{B}}$ and $\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{B}}$

Given that $\mathrm{m}_2=3 \mathrm{~m}_1$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{m}_1+3 \mathrm{~m}_1=\frac{-2 \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{~B}}=-6 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}_1=\frac{-3}{2}$$

and $m_1 \times 3 m_1=\frac{A}{B}=K \Rightarrow 3 m_1^2=K \Rightarrow K=\frac{27}{4}$

content: Let $\mathrm{P} \equiv(-5,0), \mathrm{Q} \equiv(0,0)$ and $\mathrm{R} \equiv(2,2 \sqrt{3})$ be three points. Then the equation of the bisector of the angle $P Q R$ is
explanation:
Slope of $\mathrm{QR}=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}-0}{3-0}=\sqrt{3}$ i.e., $\theta=60^{\circ}$

Clearly, $\angle \mathrm{PQR}=120^{\circ}$

OQ is the angle bisector of the angle PQR , so line OQ makes $120^{\circ}$ with the positive direction of X -axis.

Therefore, equation of the bisector of $\angle \mathrm{PQR}$ is $y=\tan 120^{\circ} x \Rightarrow y=-\sqrt{3} x \Rightarrow \sqrt{3} x+y=0$

content: If the length of the perpendicular to a line from the origin is $2 \sqrt{2}$ units, which makes an angle of $135^{\circ}$ with the X -axis, then the equation of line is
explanation:
Given that the perpendicular to the line makes an angle of $135^{\circ}$ with the X -axis.

$\therefore \quad$ Slope of perpendicular $=\tan \left(135^{\circ}\right)=1$

$\therefore \quad$ Slope of the required line $=1$, and it passes through the second quadrant.

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of the line is of the form

$$\begin{aligned} & y=x+c \\ & \text { i.e., } x-y+c=0 \end{aligned}$$

Distance of this line from the origin is $2 \sqrt{2}$ units.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2 \sqrt{2}=\left|\frac{0-0+c}{\sqrt{(1)^2+(-1)^2}}\right| \\ & \therefore \\ \therefore & 2 \sqrt{2}=\left|\frac{c}{\sqrt{2}}\right| \end{array}$$

$\therefore \quad c=4 \quad \ldots[c=-4$ is not possible as line passes through second quadrant]

content: The number of integer values of $m$, for which $x$-coordinate of the point of intersection of the lines $3 x+4 y=9$ and $y=m x+1$ is also an integer, is
explanation:
By solving $3 x+4 y=9, y=m x+1$, we get $$ x=\frac{5}{3+4 \mathrm{~m}} .$$

Now, $x$ is an integer, if $3+4 \mathrm{~m}=1,-1,5,-5$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{m}=\frac{-2}{4}, \frac{-4}{4}, \frac{2}{4}, \frac{-8}{4}$.

Since, $m=\frac{-2}{4}, \frac{2}{4}$ do not give integral values of $m$.

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{m}$ has two integer values.

content: Let a line intersect the co-ordinate axes in points $A$ and $B$ such that the area of the triangle $O A B$ is 12 sq. units. If the line passes through the point $(2,3)$, then the equation of the line is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Area of } \triangle A O B=12 \text { sq. units }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{1}{2} a b=12 \\ \therefore & a b=24 \\ \therefore & b=\frac{24}{a}\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

Equation of line in point slope form is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-a=\frac{-a}{b}(x-0) \\ & y-a=\frac{-a x}{\frac{24}{a}}\quad\text{...[from (i)]} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &y-\mathrm{a}=\frac{-\mathrm{a}^2 x}{24}\quad\text{.... (ii)}\\ &\text { Since line passes through }(2,3) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & 3-a=\frac{-a^2(2)}{24} \\ & \Rightarrow 3-a=\frac{-a^2}{12} \\ & \Rightarrow a^2-12 a+36=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(a-6)^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow a=6 \\ \therefore \quad & b=\frac{24}{6}=4\quad\text{.... [from (i)]} \end{array}$$

Required equation of line is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-6=\frac{-36 x}{24} \quad\text{.... [from (ii)]}\\ & 2 y-12=-3 x \\ & \Rightarrow 3 x+2 y-12=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 x+2 y=12 \end{aligned}$$

content: $\triangle \mathrm{OAB}$ is formed by the lines $x^2-4 x y+y^2=0$ and the line $A B$. The equation of line $A B$ is $2 x+3 y-1=0$. Then the equation of the median of the triangle drawn from the origin is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Let } \mathrm{D} \text { be the midpoint of line } \mathrm{AB} \text {. }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \mathrm{A}=\left(x_1, y_1\right) \mathrm{B}=\left(x_2, y_2\right) \\ \therefore & \mathrm{D} \equiv\left(\frac{x_1+x_2}{2}, \frac{y_1+y_2}{2}\right) \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

Combined equation of side OA and OB is $$x^2-4 x y+y^2=0$$

and equation of line AB is $2 x+3 y-1=0$

$\therefore \quad$ Points A, B satisfy $x^2-4 x y+y^2=0$ and $2 x+3 y-1=0$

$$\Rightarrow x=\frac{1-3 y}{2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Substituting above value in } x^2-4 x y+y^2=0\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \left(\frac{1-3 y}{2}\right)^2-4\left(\frac{1-3 y}{2}\right) y+y^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(1-3 y)^2-8 y(1-3 y)+4 y^2=0 \\ & 1-6 y+9 y^2-8 y+24 y^2+4 y^2=0 \\ \therefore \quad & 37 y^2-14 y+1=0 \\ & \text { Sum of roots }=\frac{-\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{14}{37} \\ & y_1+y_2=\frac{14}{37} \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

$$y \text {-coordinate of } \mathrm{D}=\frac{y_1+y_2}{2}=\frac{7}{37}$$

Since point D lies on line AB

$\therefore \quad$ Substituting $y=\frac{7}{37}$ in $2 x+3 y-1=0$

$\Rightarrow 2 x+3\left(\frac{7}{37}\right)-1=0$

$\Rightarrow 2 x+\frac{21}{37}-1=0$

$\Rightarrow x=\frac{8}{37}$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{D} \equiv\left(\frac{8}{37}, \frac{7}{37}\right)$

Equation of median AD is

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x-0}{0-\frac{8}{37}}=\frac{y-0}{0-\frac{7}{37}} \\ & \Rightarrow 7 x-8 y=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The joint equation of pair of lines through the origin and making an angle of $\frac{\pi}{6}$ with the line $3 x+y-6=0$ is
explanation:
The slope of the line $3 x+y-6=0$ is $\mathrm{m}_1=-3$.

Let $m$ be the slope of one of the lines making an angle $\frac{\pi}{6}$ with $3 x+y-6=0$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \tan \frac{\pi}{6}=\left|\frac{m-m_1}{1+m_1}\right| \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\left|\frac{m-(-3)}{1+m(-3)}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\left|\frac{m+3}{1-3 m}\right| \end{aligned}$$

Squaring on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & (1-3 m)^2=3(m+3)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 6 m^2-24 m-26=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 m^2-12 m-13=0 \end{aligned}$$

This is the auxiliary equation of two lines and their joint equation is obtained by putting $\mathrm{m}=\frac{y}{x}$.

$$\begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \text { The joint equation of the lines is }\\ &\begin{aligned} & 3\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2-12\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-13=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 13 x^2+12 x y-3 y^2=0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: A line $4 x+y=1$ passes through the point $\mathrm{A}(2,-7)$ meets the line BC whose equation is $3 x-4 y+1=0$ at the point $B$. The equation of the line $A C$ so that $A B=A C$ is
explanation:
Slopes of $A B$ and $B C$ are $-4$ and $\frac{3}{4}$ respectively.

Let $\alpha$ be the angle between AB and BC.

Then, $\tan \alpha=\left|\frac{-4-\frac{3}{4}}{1-4\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)}\right|=\frac{19}{8}\quad\text{.... (i)}$

Since $A B=A C$

$\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{ABC}=\angle \mathrm{ACB}=\alpha$

$\therefore \quad$ the line $A C$ also makes an angle $\alpha$ with $B C$.

If $m$ is the slope of the line $A C$, then its equation is $y+7=\mathrm{m}(x-2)$

Now, $\tan \alpha= \pm\left[\frac{m-\frac{3}{4}}{1+m \cdot \frac{3}{4}}\right]$

$\Rightarrow \frac{19}{8}= \pm \frac{4 \mathrm{~m}-3}{4+3 \mathrm{~m}}\quad \text{...[From (i)]}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{m}=-4$ or $-\frac{52}{89}$

But slope of $A B$ is -4 , so slope of $A C$ is $-\frac{52}{89}$.

Therefore, the equation of line AC given by (ii) is $52 x+89 y+519=0$.

content: If an equation $h x y+g x+f y+c=0$ represents a pair of lines, then
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given equation of pair of lines is }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{h} x y+\mathrm{g} x+\mathrm{f} y+\mathrm{c}=0 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{~A}=0, \mathrm{~B}=0, \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{c}, \mathrm{~F}=\frac{\mathrm{f}}{2}, \mathrm{G}=\frac{\mathrm{g}}{2}, \mathrm{H}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore & \left|\begin{array}{lll} A & H & G \\ H & B & F \\ G & F & C \end{array}\right|=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \left|\begin{array}{lll} 0 & \frac{h}{2} & \frac{g}{2} \\ \frac{h}{2} & 0 & \frac{f}{2} \\ \frac{g}{2} & \frac{f}{2} & c \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-h}{2}\left[\frac{\mathrm{ch}}{2}-\frac{g f}{4}\right]+\frac{g}{2}\left[\frac{h f}{4}\right]=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-h^2 c}{4}+\frac{h g f}{8}+\frac{g h f}{8}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-h^2 c}{4}+\frac{h g f}{4}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow h^2 c=h g f \\ & \Rightarrow h c=g f \end{aligned}$$

content: The straight line, $2 x-3 y+17=0$ is perpendicular to the line passing through the points $(7,17)$ and $(15, \beta)$, then $\beta$ equals
explanation:
Slope of $2 x-3 y+17=0$ is $\frac{2}{3}$

Slope of line joining points $(7,17)$ and $(15, \beta)$ is

$$\frac{\beta-17}{15-7}=\frac{\beta-17}{8}$$

Since, lines are perpendicular

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{\beta-17}{8}=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow \beta-17=-12 \quad \Rightarrow \beta=5 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\mathrm{O}(0,0), \mathrm{A}(1,2)$ and $\mathrm{B}(3,4)$ are the vertices of triangle OAB , then the joint equation of the altitude and median drawn from O is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\mathrm{OD} \text { is the median }\\ &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \mathrm{D} \equiv\left(\frac{1+3}{2}, \frac{2+4}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{D} \equiv(2,3) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Equation of OD is $y=m x$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow y=\frac{3}{2} x \\ & \Rightarrow 3 x-2 y=0 \end{aligned}$$

Slope of line $A B=\frac{2}{2}=1$

Given, $\mathrm{OE} \perp \mathrm{AB}$

$\therefore \quad$ Slope of $\mathrm{OE}=-1$

Equation of OE is $y=\mathrm{mx}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow y=-x \\ & \Rightarrow x+y=0 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Joint equation of median and altitude is

$$\begin{aligned} & (3 x-2 y)(x+y)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 x^2+x y-2 y^2=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The combined equation of two lines through the origin and making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the line $3 x+y=0$, is
explanation:
Given line $3 x+y=0 \Rightarrow$ slope $=-3$

Let the slope of required line be $m$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \tan 45^{\circ}=\left|\frac{\mathrm{m}+3}{1-3 \mathrm{~m}}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow 1=\left|\frac{\mathrm{m}+3}{1-3 \mathrm{~m}}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~m}^2-3 \mathrm{~m}-2=0\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Since the line passes through origin, its equation is $y=m x$

$$\Rightarrow \mathrm{m}=\frac{y}{x}$$

Substituting the value of $m$ in equation (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 2\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2-3\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 y^2-3 x y-2 x^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x^2+3 x y-2 y^2=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The acute angle between the lines $x \cos 30^{\circ}+y \sin 30^{\circ}=3$ and $x \cos 60^{\circ}+y \sin 60^{\circ}=5$ is
explanation:
Given equations of lines, $x \cos 30^{\circ}+y \sin 30^{\circ}=3$ and $x \cos 60^{\circ}+y \sin 60^{\circ}=5$

$$\Rightarrow \sqrt{3} x+y=6 \text { and } x+\sqrt{3} y=10$$

$\therefore \quad$ Slope of line $\sqrt{3} x+y=6=\mathrm{m}_1=-\sqrt{3}$

Slope of line $x+\sqrt{3} y=10=\mathrm{m}_2=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{3}}$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \tan \theta & =\left|\frac{m_1-m_2}{1+m_1 m_2}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{-\sqrt{3}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}{1+(-\sqrt{3}) \times\left(\frac{-1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{-3+1}{2 \sqrt{3}}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{-2}{2 \sqrt{3}}\right| \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \tan \theta & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\ \Rightarrow \theta & =30^{\circ} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $4 a b=3 h^2$, then the ratio of the slope of lines represented by $a x^2+2 \mathrm{~h} x y+\mathrm{b} y^2=0$ is
explanation:
Here, $m_1+m_2=\frac{-2 h}{b}\quad\text{.... (i)}$

and $\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{b}}$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad\left(m_1-m_2\right)^2 & =\left(m_1+m_2\right)^2-4 m_1 m_2 \\ & =\frac{4 h^2-4 a b}{b^2} \\ & =\frac{4 h^2-3 h^2}{b^2} \ldots\left[\because 4 a b=3 h^2 \text { (given) }\right] \\ & =\frac{h^2}{b^2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{m}_1-\mathrm{m}_2=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{~b}}\quad\text{.... (ii)}$$

On solving (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \mathrm{m}_1=\frac{-\mathrm{h}}{2 \mathrm{~b}} \text { and } \mathrm{m}_2=\frac{-3 \mathrm{~h}}{2 \mathrm{~b}} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{~m}_1: \mathrm{m}_2=1: 3 \end{aligned}$$

content: The line L given by $\frac{x}{5}+\frac{y}{b}=1$ passes through the point $(13,32)$. The line K is parallel to line L and has the equation $\frac{x}{c}+\frac{y}{3}=1$. Then the distance between L and K is _________ units.
explanation:
Line L passes through $(13,32)$.

$$\begin{array}{r} \therefore \quad \frac{13}{5}+\frac{32}{b}=1 \\ \Rightarrow b=-20 \end{array}$$

So, equation of $L$ is $\frac{x}{5}-\frac{y}{20}=1 \Rightarrow 4 x-y=20$

Slope of $L$ is $\mathrm{m}_1=4$.

Slope of $\frac{x}{c}+\frac{y}{3}=1$ is $\mathrm{m}_2=\frac{3}{\mathrm{c}}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow-\frac{3}{c}=4 \\ & \Rightarrow c=\frac{3}{4} \end{aligned}$$

Equation of line K is $-\frac{4 x}{3}+\frac{y}{3}=1$

$$\Rightarrow 4 x-y=-3$$

Distance between $L$ and $K$ is $\left|\frac{20+3}{\sqrt{16+1}}\right|=\frac{23}{\sqrt{17}}$

content: The equation of the line passing through the point of intersection of the lines $3 x-y=5$ and $x+3 y=1$ and making equal intercepts on the axes is
explanation:
Required equation passes through point of intersection of lines $3 x-y=5$ and $x+3 y=1$

$\therefore \quad$ Point of intersection $=\left(\frac{16}{10}, \frac{-2}{10}\right)$

Equation of line in double intercept form is $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$

But $\mathrm{a}=\mathrm{b}$ ...[Given]

So, equation of line is $x+y=\mathrm{a}$

Since line passes through $\left(\frac{16}{10}, \frac{-2}{10}\right)$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \frac{16}{10}+\frac{-2}{10}=a \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{14}{10}=a \\ & \Rightarrow a=\frac{7}{5} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ The required equation of line is $x+y=\frac{7}{5}$

$$\Rightarrow 5 x+5 y-7=0$$

content: The equation of pair of lines $y=p x$ and $y=q x$ can be written as $(y-p x)(y-q x)=0$. Then the equation of the pair of the angle bisectors of the lines $x^2-4 x y-5 y^2=0$ is
explanation:
Equation of angle bisector of two lines whose general equation is $a x^2+2 \mathrm{~h} x y+b y^2=0$ is $$\frac{x^2-y^2}{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}}=\frac{x y}{\mathrm{~h}}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Comparing given equation $x^2-4 x y-5 y^2=0$ with $a x^2+2 \mathrm{~h} x y+b y^2=0$, we get $$\mathrm{a}=1, \mathrm{~b}=-5, \mathrm{~h}=-2$$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of angle bisectors is

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x^2-y^2}{1-(-5)}=\frac{x y}{-2} \\ & \Rightarrow x^2-y^2=-3 x y \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+3 x y-y^2=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The number of possible distinct straight lines passing through $(2,3)$ and forming a triangle with co-ordinate axes whose area is 12 sq . units are,
explanation:
Let the equation of the line be

$$\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$$

Since line passes through the point $(2,3)$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{2}{a}+\frac{3}{b}=1 \\ \therefore \quad & 2 b+3 a=a b\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

As per the given condition, Area of the triangle $=12$ sq. units

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{1}{2}|a b|=12 \\ & \therefore \quad a b= \pm 24 \end{aligned}$$

Case I :

$$a b=24\quad\text{.... (ii)}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2 b+3 a=24 \quad\text{....[from (i)]}\\ \therefore & 2\left(\frac{24}{a}\right)^2+3 a=24 \quad\text{....[from (ii)]}\\ \therefore & 16+a^2=8 a \\ \therefore & a^2-8 a+16=0 \\ \therefore & a=4 \\ \therefore & b=6 \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Case II: } \\ & a b=-24 \\ & \therefore \quad 2\left(\frac{-24}{a}\right)+3 a=-24 \quad\text{.... [from (i) and (ii)]}\\ & \therefore \quad a^2+8 a-16=0 \\ & \therefore \quad a=\frac{-8 \pm \sqrt{64+64}}{2}=-4 \pm 4 \sqrt{2} \\ \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad\mathrm{b}$ will also have 2 values.

$\therefore \quad$ The required number of lines is 3.

content: The line L given by $\frac{x}{5}+\frac{y}{b}=1$ passes through the point $(13,32)$. The line K is parallel to L and has the equation $\frac{x}{c}+\frac{y}{3}=1$. Then the distance between $L$ and $K$ is
explanation:
Line L passes through $(13,32)$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \frac{13}{5}+\frac{32}{b}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow b=-20 \end{aligned}$$

So, equation of $L$ is $\frac{x}{5}-\frac{y}{20}=1 \Rightarrow 4 x-y=20$

Slope of $L$ is $\mathrm{m}_1=4$.

Slope of $\frac{x}{c}+\frac{y}{3}=1$ is $\mathrm{m}_2=-\frac{3}{c}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow-\frac{3}{c}=4 \\ & \Rightarrow c=-\frac{3}{4} \end{aligned}$$

Equation of line K is $-\frac{4 x}{3}+\frac{y}{3}=1$

$$\Rightarrow 4 x-y=-3$$

Distance between $L$ and $K$ is $\left|\frac{20+3}{\sqrt{16+1}}\right|=\frac{23}{\sqrt{17}}$

content: The diagonals of a parallelogram $A B C D$ are along the lines $x+3 y=4$ and $6 x-2 y=7$. Then ABCD must be a
explanation:
Slope of $x+3 y=4$ is $\mathrm{m}_1=-\frac{1}{3}$

Slope of $6 x-2 y=7$ is $m_2=3$

Here, $m_1 \cdot m_2=-1$

$\therefore \quad$ The diagonals are perpendicular to each other.

$\therefore \quad$ Parallelogram ABCD is a rhombus.

content: If the equation $7 x^2-14 x y+p y^2-12 x+q y-4=0$ represents a pair of parallel lines then the value of $\sqrt{p^2+q^2-p q}$ is
explanation:
Given equation of pair of lines is

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & 7 x^2-14 x y+p y^2-12 x+q y-4=0 \\ \therefore \quad & a=7, b=p, c=-4, h=-7, g=-6, f=\frac{q}{2} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { The lines are parallel. }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \mathrm{h}^2=\mathrm{ab} \\ & \Rightarrow(-7)^2=7 \mathrm{p} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}=7 \\ & \text { Now, } a b c+2 \mathrm{fgh}-\mathrm{af}^2-\mathrm{bg}^2-\mathrm{ch}^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 7(7)(-4)+2\left(\frac{q}{2}\right)(-6)(-7) \\ & -7\left(\frac{\mathrm{q}}{2}\right)^2-7(-6)^2-(-4)(-7)^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-196+42 q-\frac{7 q^2}{4}-252+196=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{q}^2-24 \mathrm{q}+144=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\mathrm{q}-12)^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{q}=12 \\ & \therefore \quad \sqrt{p^2+q^2-p q}=\sqrt{49+144-84}=\sqrt{109} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The slopes of the lines given by $x^2+2 h x y+2 y^2=0$ are in the ratio $1: 2$, then $h$ is
explanation:
Given equation of pair of lines is

$$\begin{aligned} & x^2+2 h x y+2 y^2=0 \\ \therefore \quad & A=1, B=2, H=h \end{aligned}$$

Let the slopes of the lines be $\mathrm{m}_1, \mathrm{~m}_2$.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \mathrm{m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2=\frac{-2 h}{2}, \mathrm{~m}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \text { Given } \frac{\mathrm{m}_1}{\mathrm{~m}_2}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}_1=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}_2 \times \mathrm{m}_2=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}_2^2=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}_2= \pm 1 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{~m}_1= \pm \frac{1}{2} . \end{array}$$

Now, $m_1+m_2=\frac{-2 h}{2}$

$\therefore \quad$ when $\mathrm{m}_1=\frac{1}{2}, \mathrm{~m}_2=1$

$$\begin{array}{ll} & m_1+m_2=\frac{-2 h}{2} \\ \therefore \quad & h=\frac{-3}{2} \\ & \text { When } m_1=\frac{-1}{2}, m_2=-1 \\ \therefore \quad & m_1+m_2=\frac{-2 h}{2} \\ & \frac{-1}{2}-1=-\frac{2 h}{2} \\ \therefore \quad & h=\frac{3}{2} \end{array}$$

content: If two lines $x+(a-1) y=1 \quad$ and $2 x+a^2 y=1(a \in R-\{0,1\})$ are perpendicular, then the distance of their point of intersection from the origin is
explanation:
Given equations of lines

$$\begin{aligned} & x+(\mathrm{a}-1) y=1 \\ & 2 x+\mathrm{a}^2 y=1 \end{aligned}$$

Slope of $x+(a-1) y=1$ is $\frac{-1}{a-1}$

and slope of $2 x+a^2 y=1$ is $\frac{-2}{a^2}$

Given lines are perpendicular

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \text { Product of slope }=-1 \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{-1}{(a-1)} \times \frac{-2}{a^2}=-1 \\ & \\ & \quad \Rightarrow \frac{2}{a^2(a-1)}=-1 \\ & \\ & \Rightarrow a^3-a^2=-2 \\ & \\ & \Rightarrow a^3-a^2+2=0 \\ & \\ & \Rightarrow(a+1)\left(a^2-2 a+2\right)=0 \\ & \\ & \Rightarrow a=-1, a^2-2 a+2=0 \\ & \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a a^2-2 a+2 \neq 0 \ldots[\because a \in R-\{0,1\}] \\ & \therefore \quad a=-1 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Equations will be

$$\begin{aligned} & x-2 y=1 \\ & 2 x+y=1 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Point of intersection is $\left(\frac{3}{5}, \frac{-1}{5}\right)$

Distance of point of intersection from origin

$$\begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{\left(0-\frac{3}{5}\right)^2+\left(0-\left(\frac{-1}{5}\right)\right)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{9}{25}+\frac{1}{25}} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{10}{25}}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{5}} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$P \equiv(-3,0), Q \equiv(0,0)$$ and $$R \equiv(3,3 \sqrt{3})$$ be three points. Then the equation of the bisector of the angle $$\mathrm{PQR}$$ is
explanation:
Slope of $$Q R=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}-0}{3-0}=\sqrt{3}$$ i.e., $$\theta=60^{\circ}$$

Clearly, $$\angle \mathrm{PQR}=120^{\circ}$$

$$\mathrm{OQ}$$ is the angle bisector of the angle $$\mathrm{PQR}$$, so line OQ makes $$120^{\circ}$$ with the positive direction of $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis.

Therefore, equation of the bisector of $$\angle \mathrm{PQR}$$ is $$y=\tan 120^{\circ} x \Rightarrow y=-\sqrt{3} x \Rightarrow \sqrt{3} x+y=0$$

content: The centroid of the triangle formed by the lines $$x+3 y=10$$ and $$6 x^2+x y-y^2=0$$ is
explanation:
Lines represented by the equation $$6 x^2+x y-y^2=0$$ are $$y=3 x$$ and $$y=-2 x$$ The co-ordinates of the vertices of the triangle formed by above lines with $$x+3 y=10$$ are $$(0,0),(1,3)$$ and $$(-2,4)$$.

$$\therefore \quad \text { Centroid }=\left(\frac{0+1-2}{3}, \frac{0+3+4}{3}\right)=\left(\frac{-1}{3}, \frac{7}{3}\right) $$

content: $$\mathrm{p}$$ is the length of perpendicular from the origin to the line whose intercepts on the axes are a and $$\mathrm{b}$$ respectively, then $$\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}^2}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{~b}^2}$$ equals
explanation:
Let the equation of the line be

$$\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$$

According to the given condition,

$$\begin{aligned} & p=\left|\frac{a b}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{a^2+b^2}{a^2 b^2}=\frac{1}{p^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{a^2}+\frac{1}{b^2}=\frac{1}{p^2} \end{aligned}$$

content: The perpendiculars are drawn to lines $$L_1$$ and $$L_2$$ from the origin making an angle $$\frac{\pi}{4}$$ and $$\frac{3 \pi}{4}$$ respectively with positive direction of $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis. If both the lines are at unit distance from the origin, then their joint equation is
explanation:
Equation of line $$\mathrm{L}_1$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & x \cos \frac{\pi}{4}+y \sin \frac{\pi}{4}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{y}{\sqrt{2}}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y-\sqrt{2}=0 \end{aligned}$$

Equation of line $$L_2$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & x \cos \frac{3 \pi}{4}+y \sin \frac{3 \pi}{4}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-x}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{y}{\sqrt{2}}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow x-y+\sqrt{2}=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ The joint equation of the lines is

$$\begin{aligned} & (x+y-\sqrt{2})(x-y+\sqrt{2})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2-y^2+2 \sqrt{2} y-2=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$P Q R$$ be a right angled isosceles triangle, right angled at $$Q(2,1)$$. If the equation of the line $$P R$$ is $$2 x+y=3$$, then the combined equation representing the pair of lines $$P Q$$ and $$Q R$$ is
explanation:
The equations of $$P Q$$ and $$P R$$ are given by

$$y-1=\frac{-2 \mp \tan 45^{\circ}}{1 \pm(-2) \tan 45^{\circ}}(x-2)$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & y-1=\left(\frac{-2 \mp 1}{1 \pm 2}\right)(x-2) \\ \Rightarrow & y-1=-\frac{1}{3}(x-2) \\ \text { and } & y-1=3(x-2) \\ \Rightarrow & x+3 y=5 \text { and } 3 x-y=5 \end{array}$$

The combined equation of these two lines is

$$\begin{aligned} (x+3 y-5)(3 x-y-5) & =0 \\ \Rightarrow 3 x^2-3 y^2+8 x y-20 x-10 y+25 & =0 \end{aligned}$$

content: $$P S$$ is the median of the triangle with vertices at $$P(2,2), Q(6,-1)$$ and $$R(7,3)$$, then the intercepts on the coordinate axes of the line passing through point $$(1,-1)$$ and parallel to PS are respectively
explanation:
Since, $$S$$ is mid-point of $$Q$$ and $$R$$.

$$\therefore \quad S=\left(\frac{7+6}{2}, \frac{3-1}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{13}{2}, 1\right)$$

Slope of $$P S=m=\frac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}=\frac{2-1}{2-\frac{13}{2}}=\frac{-2}{9}$$

$$\therefore$$ Equation of line passing through point $$(1,-1)$$ and parallel to $$P S$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} y+1 & =-\frac{2}{9}(x-1) \\ 9 y+9 & =-2 x+2 \Rightarrow 2 x+9 y=-7 \end{aligned}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{\left(\frac{-7}{2}\right)}+\frac{y}{\left(\frac{-7}{9}\right)}=1$$

$$\text { Hence, } x \text {-intercept }=-\frac{7}{2} \text { and } y \text {-intercept }=-\frac{7}{9}$$

content: If the angle between the lines given by $$x^2-3 x y+\lambda y^2+3 x-5 y+2=0 ; \lambda \geq 0$$ is $$\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)$$, then the value of $$\lambda$$ is
explanation:
Given equation of pair of lines is

$$x^2-3 x y+\lambda y^2+3 x-5 y+2=0$$

Here, $$\mathrm{a}=1, \mathrm{~b}=\lambda, \mathrm{c}=2, \mathrm{f}=\frac{-5}{2}, \mathrm{~g}=\frac{3}{2}, \mathrm{~h}=\frac{-3}{2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) \Rightarrow \tan \theta=\frac{1}{3} \\ & \text { Since } \tan \theta=\left|\frac{2 \sqrt{h^2-a b}}{a+b}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}=\left|\frac{2 \sqrt{\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)^2-\lambda}}{\lambda+1}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow(\lambda+1)^2=9(9-4 \lambda) \Rightarrow \lambda^2+38 \lambda-80=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\lambda+40)(\lambda-2)=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=2 \quad \ldots[\because \lambda \geq 0] \end{aligned}$$

content: The base of an equilateral triangle is represented by the equation $$2 x-y-1=0$$ and its vertex is $$(1,2)$$, then the length (in units) of the side of the triangle is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} A D & =\left|\frac{2-2-1}{\sqrt{2^2+(-1)^2}}\right| \\\\ & =\left|\frac{-1}{\sqrt{5}}\right| \\\\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { In } \triangle A B D, \tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{A D}{B D} \\\\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{3}=\frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}}{B D} \\\\ & \Rightarrow B D=\frac{1}{\sqrt{15}} \\\\ & \therefore B C=2 B D=\frac{2}{\sqrt{15}} \end{aligned}$$

content: A line is drawn through the point $$(1,2)$$ to meet the co-ordinate axes at $$\mathrm{P}$$ and $$\mathrm{Q}$$ such that it forms a $$\triangle \mathrm{OPQ}$$, where $$\mathrm{O}$$ is the origin. If the area of $$\triangle \mathrm{OPQ}$$ is least, then the slope of the line $$\mathrm{PQ}$$ is
explanation:
The equation of line $$\mathrm{PQ}$$ passing through $$(1,2)$$ is $$y-2=\mathrm{m}(x-1)$$

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{A}(\triangle \mathrm{OPQ}) & =\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{OP} \times \mathrm{OQ} \\\\ = & \frac{1}{2}\left(1-\frac{2}{\mathrm{~m}}\right)(2-\mathrm{m}) \\\\ = & \frac{1}{2}\left(4-\mathrm{m}-\frac{4}{\mathrm{~m}}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & A=2-\frac{m}{2}-\frac{2}{m} \\\\ \therefore & \frac{d A}{d m}=-\frac{1}{2}+\frac{2}{m^2} \\\\ & \text { Now, } \frac{d A}{d m}=0 \\\\ \Rightarrow & -\frac{1}{2}+\frac{2}{m^2}=0 \\\\ \Rightarrow m^2 =4 \\\\ \Rightarrow m = \pm 2 \\\\ & \frac{d^2 A}{\mathrm{dm}^2}=-\frac{4}{m^3} \\\\ & \text{At} m=2, \\\\ & \frac{d^2 A}{d^2}<0 \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { At } m=-2, \\\\ & \frac{d^2 A}{d m^2}>0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore\quad$$Area of $$\triangle \mathrm{OPQ}$$ will be least at $$\mathrm{m}=-2$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ Slope of the line $$P Q=-2$$

content: If the pair of lines given by $$(x \cos \alpha+y \sin \alpha)^2=\left(x^2+y^2\right) \sin ^2 \alpha$$ are perpendicular to each other, then $$\alpha$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{gathered} \quad(x \cos \alpha+y \sin \alpha)^2=\left(x^2+y^2\right) \sin ^2 \alpha \\ \therefore \quad x^2 \cos ^2 \alpha+y^2 \sin ^2 \alpha+2 x y \sin \alpha \cos \alpha \\ \quad=x^2 \sin ^2 \alpha+y^2 \sin ^2 \alpha \\ \therefore \quad x^2\left(\cos ^2 \alpha-\sin ^2 \alpha\right)+2 x y \sin \alpha \cos \alpha=0 \end{gathered}$$

This represents a pair of straight lines where $$\mathrm{a}=\cos ^2 \alpha-\sin ^2 \alpha, \mathrm{h}=\sin \alpha \cos \alpha$$ and $$\mathrm{b}=0$$

As lines are perpendicular, we get $$a+b=0$$

$$\therefore \cos ^2 \alpha=\sin ^2 \alpha \Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{\pi}{4}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{i}}$$ are possible values of $$\mathrm{k}$$ for which lines $$\mathrm{k} x+2 y+2=0,2 x+\mathrm{k} y+3=0$$ and $$3 x+3 y+\mathrm{k}=0$$ are concurrent, then $$\sum \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{i}}$$ has the value
explanation:
If three lines $$\mathrm{a}_1 x+\mathrm{b}_1 y+\mathrm{c}_1=0, \mathrm{a}_2+\mathrm{b}_2 y+\mathrm{c}_2=0$$ and $$\mathrm{a}_3 x+\mathrm{b}_3 y+\mathrm{c}_3=0$$ are concurrent, then $$\left|\begin{array}{lll}a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \\ a_3 & b_3 & c_3\end{array}\right|=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & \left|\begin{array}{lll} k & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & k & 3 \\ 3 & 3 & k \end{array}\right|=0 \\ \Rightarrow & k\left(k^2-9\right)-2(2 k-9)+2(6-3 k)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & k^3-9 k-4 k+18+12-6 k=0 \\ \Rightarrow & k^3-19 k+30=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (k-2)\left(k^2+2 k-15\right)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (k-2)(k+5)(k-3)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & k_1=2, k_2=-5 \text { and } k_3=3 \\ \Rightarrow & \sum k_i=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The co-ordinates of the points on the line $$2 x-y=5$$ which are the distance of 1 unit from the line $$3 x+4 y=5$$ are
explanation:
Let $$\left(x_1, y_1\right)$$ be the required point

$$\therefore \quad 2 x_1-y_1=5$$ ..... (i)

Also, $$\left(x_1, y_1\right)$$ is at the distance of 1 unit from line $$3 x+4 y=5$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 1=\left|\frac{3 x_1+4 y_1-5}{\sqrt{9+16}}\right| \\ \therefore & \pm 5=3 x_1+4 y_1-5 \\ \therefore & 3 x_1+4 y_1-5=5 \quad \text { or } 3 x_1+4 y_1-5=-5 \\ \therefore & 3 x_1+4 y_1=10 \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{array}$$

or

$$3 x_1+4 y_1=0$$ .... (iii)

Solving equations (i) and (ii), we get

$$x_1=\frac{30}{11}$$ and $$y_1=\frac{5}{11}$$

Solving equation (i) and (iii), we get

$$x_1=\frac{20}{11}$$ and $$y_1=\frac{-15}{11}$$

$$\therefore\left(\frac{30}{11}, \frac{5}{11}\right)$$ and $$\left(\frac{20}{11}, \frac{-15}{11}\right)$$ are the required points.

content: Let $$\mathrm{PQR}$$ be a right angled isosceles triangle, right angled at $$\mathrm{P}(2,1)$$. If the equation of the line $$\mathrm{QR}$$ is $$2 x+y=3$$, then the equation representing the pair of lines $$P Q$$ and $$P R$$ is
explanation:
Slope of $$\mathrm{QR}=-2$$.

Slope of $$\mathrm{PQ}=\mathrm{m}_1$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \tan 45^{\circ}=\left|\frac{m_1+2}{1+m_1(-2)}\right| \\\\ & \Rightarrow 1=\left|\frac{m_1+2}{1-2 m_1}\right| \\\\ & \Rightarrow m_1=-\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Equation of $$\mathrm{PQ}$$ passing through point $$\mathrm{P}(2,1)$$ and having slope $$\frac{-1}{3}$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-1=-\frac{1}{3}(x-2) \\\\ & \Rightarrow 3(y-1)+(x-2)=0\quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Slope of $$\mathrm{PR}=\mathrm{m}_2=3\quad ...[\because PQ \bot PR]$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ equation of $$\mathrm{PR}$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & y-1=3(x-2) \\ & \Rightarrow(y-1)-3(x-2)=0 \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ The joint equation of the lines is

$$\begin{aligned} & {[3(y-1)+(x-2)][(y-1)-3(x-2)]=0} \\\\ & \Rightarrow 3(y-1)^2-8(y-1)(x-2)-3(x-2)^2=0 \\\\ & \Rightarrow 3\left(x^2-4 x+4\right)+8(x y-x-2 y+2) \\\\ & \quad-3\left(y^2-2 y+1\right)=0 \\\\ & \Rightarrow 3 x^2-3 y^2+8 x y-20 x-10 y+25=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The joint equation of the lines pair of lines passing through the point $$(3,-2)$$ and perpendicular to the lines $$5 x^2+2 x y-3 y^2=0$$ is
explanation:
Joint equation of the liens passing through the point $$\left(x_1, y_1\right)$$ and perpendicular to the lines $$\mathrm{a} x^2+2 \mathrm{~h} x y+\mathrm{b} y^2=0$$ is:

$$\mathrm{b}\left(x-x_1\right)^2-2 \mathrm{~h}\left(x-x_1\right)\left(y-y_1\right)+\mathrm{a}\left(y-y_1\right)^2=0$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Equation of the required line is:

$$\begin{aligned} -3(x-3)^2-2(x-3)(y+2)+5(y+2)^2=0 \\ \therefore \quad-3\left(x^2-6 x+9\right)-2(x y+2 x-3 y-6)+5\left(y^2+4 y+4\right)=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad-3 x^2+18 x-27-2 x y-4 x+6 y+12+5 y^2 \\ & \quad+20 y+20=0 \\ & \therefore \quad 3 x^2+2 x y-5 y^2-14 x-26 y-5=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: If the angle between the lines represented by the equation $$x^2+\lambda x y-y^2 \tan ^2 \theta=0$$ is $$2 \theta$$, then the value of $$\lambda$$ is
explanation:
Given equation of pair of lines is

$$\begin{array}{ll} & x^2+\lambda x y-y^2 \tan ^2 \theta=0 \\ \therefore \quad & a=1, h=\frac{\lambda}{2}, \mathrm{~b}=-\tan ^2 \theta \\ \therefore \quad & \tan 2 \theta=\left|\frac{2 \sqrt{\mathrm{h}^2-\mathrm{ab}}}{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2 \tan \theta}{1-\tan ^2 \theta}=\left|\frac{2 \sqrt{\frac{\lambda^2}{4}+\tan ^2 \theta}}{1-\tan ^2 \theta}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\lambda^2}{4}+\tan ^2 \theta=\tan ^2 \theta \\ &\Rightarrow \lambda=0 \end{array}$$

content: $$\mathrm{a}$$ and $$\mathrm{b}$$ are the intercepts made by a line on the co-ordinate axes. If $$3 \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{b}$$ and the line passes through $$(1,3)$$, then the equation of the line is
explanation:
Let the equation of the line be

$$\frac{x}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{y}{\mathrm{~b}}=1$$ .... (i)

This line passes through the point $$(1,3)$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{a}+\frac{3}{b}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow b+3 a=a b \\ & \Rightarrow 2 b=a b \quad ...[\because 3a=b]\\ & \Rightarrow a=2 \\ \therefore \quad & b=3 a=6 \end{aligned}$$

Equation (i) becomes,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x}{2}+\frac{y}{6}=1 \\ & 3 x+y=6 \end{aligned}$$

content: The joint equation of a pair of lines passing through the origin and making an angle of $$\frac{\pi}{4}$$ with the line $$3 x+2 y-8=0$$ is
explanation:
The slope of line $$3 x+2 y-8=0$$ is $$m_1=\frac{-3}{2}$$

Let $$m$$ be the slope of one of the lines making angle $$\frac{\pi}{4}$$ with $$3 x+2 y-8=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \tan \frac{\pi}{4} & =\left|\frac{m-m_1}{1+m_1}\right| \\ \Rightarrow 1 & =\left|\frac{m-\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)}{1+m\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)}\right| \\ \Rightarrow 1 & =\left|\frac{2 m+3}{2-3 m}\right| \end{aligned}$$

Squaring on both sides, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & (2-3 m)^2=(2 m+3)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 5 m^2-24 m-5=0 \end{aligned}$$

This is the auxiliary equation of two lines and their joint equation is obtained by putting

$$\mathrm{m}=\frac{y}{x}$$

The joint equation of the lines is

$$\begin{aligned} & 5\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2-24\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-5=0 \\ & \text { i.e., } 5 x^2+24 x y-5 y^2=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: Two sides of a square are along the lines $$5 x-12 y+39=0$$ and $$5 x-12 y+78=0$$, then area of the square is
explanation:
Given equations of lines are $$5 x-12 y+39=0$$ and $$5 x-12 y+78=0$$.

Slope of $$5 x-12 y+39=0$$ is $$\frac{5}{12}$$

Slope of $$5 x-12 y+78=0$$ is $$\frac{5}{12}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Lines are parallel.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Distance between two parallel lines $$=\left|\frac{\mathrm{c}_1-\mathrm{c}_2}{\sqrt{\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2}}\right|$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left|\frac{39-78}{\sqrt{5^2+(-12)^2}}\right| \\ & =3 \text { units } \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Side of the square $$=3$$ units

$$\therefore \quad$$ Area of square $$=3^2=9$$ sq. units

content: The number of integral values of $$\mathrm{p}$$ in the domain $$[-5,5]$$, such that the equation $$2 x^2+4 x y-p y^2+4 x+q y+1=0$$ represents pair of lines, are
explanation:
Given equation of pair of lines is

$$2 x^2+4 x y-p y^2+4 x+q y+1=0$$

Comparing with

$$a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2+2 g x+2 f y+c=0$$, we get $$\mathrm{a}=2, \mathrm{~h}=2, \mathrm{~b}=-\mathrm{p}$$

If the given equation represents a pair of straight lines, then

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{h}^2 \geq \mathrm{ab} \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \geq-2 \mathrm{p} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \geq-\mathrm{p} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p} \geq-2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Possible values of $$\mathrm{p}$$ from domain $$[-5,5]$$ are $$-2,-1,0,1,2,3,4,5$$.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Number of integral values of $$p=8$$

content: The points $$(1,3),(5,1)$$ are opposite vertices of a diagonal of a rectangle. If the other two vertices lie on the line $$y=2 x+\mathrm{c}$$, then one of the vertex on the other diagonal is
explanation:
Diagonals of rectangle bisect each other.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Midpoint of $$(1,3)$$ and $$(5,1)$$ is $$(3,2)$$.

Also, $$y=2 x+c$$ passes through $$(3,2)$$.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad 2 & =2(3)+c \\ \therefore & c=-4 \end{array}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Other two vertices lie on $$y=2 x-4$$.

Let co-ordinates of $$\mathrm{B}$$ be $$(x, y)$$ i.e., $$(x, 2 x-4)$$

slope of $$\mathrm{AB} \times$$ slope of $$\mathrm{BC}=-1$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{2 x-4-3}{x-1}\right)\left(\frac{2 x-4-1}{x-5}\right)=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{2 x-7}{x-1}\right)\left(\frac{2 x-5}{x-5}\right)=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2-6 x+8=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=4,2 \end{aligned}$$

When $$x=4, y=4$$

When $$x=2, y=0$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Vertex of the other diagonal is $$(2,0)$$.

content: If the slope of one of the lines represented by $$a x^2+(2 a+1) x y+2 y^2=0$$ is reciprocal of the slope of the other, then the sum of squares of slopes is
explanation:
Given equation of pair of lines is

$$\begin{aligned} & a x^2+(2 \mathrm{a}+1) x y+2 y^2=0 \\ & \mathrm{~A}=\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{H}=\frac{2 \mathrm{a}+1}{2}, \mathrm{~B}=2 \end{aligned}$$

Given condition,

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{m}_1=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~m}_2} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{m}_1 \cdot \mathrm{m}_2=1 \\ & \text { Product of slopes }=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{m}_1 \cdot \mathrm{m}_2=1=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2} \\ \therefore & \quad \mathrm{a}=2 \\ & \text { Also, sum of slopes }=\frac{-2 \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{B}}=-\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{a}+1}{2}\right)=\frac{-5}{2} \\ & \text { Using }\left(\mathrm{m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2\right)^2=\mathrm{m}_1^2+\mathrm{m}_2^2+2 \mathrm{~m}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2 \\ & \left(\frac{-5}{2}\right)^2=\mathrm{m}_1^2+\mathrm{m}_2^2+2 \times 1 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{m}_1^2+\mathrm{m}_2^2=\frac{25}{4}-2 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{m}_1^2+\mathrm{m}_2^2=\frac{17}{4} \end{array}$$

content: The equation of a line, whose perpendicular distance from the origin is 7 units and the angle, which the perpendicular to the line from the origin makes, is $$120^{\circ}$$ with positive $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis, is
explanation:
Normal form of the equation of line is

$$\begin{array}{ll} & x \cos \alpha+y \sin \alpha=\mathrm{p} \\ & \text { Here, } \alpha=120^{\circ} \text { and } \mathrm{p}=7 \\ \therefore \quad & x \cos 120^{\circ}+y \sin 120^{\circ}=7 \\ \therefore & x\left(\frac{-1}{2}\right)+y\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=7 \\ \therefore & \frac{-x+\sqrt{3} y}{2}=7 \\ \therefore & -x+\sqrt{3} y=14 \\ \therefore & -x+\sqrt{3} y-14=0 \\ \therefore & x-\sqrt{3} y+14=0 \end{array}$$

content: If the distance between the parallel lines given by the equation $$x^2+4 x y+p y^2+3 x+q y-4=0$$ is $$\lambda$$, then $$\lambda^2=$$
explanation:
Given equation is

$$\begin{aligned} & x^2+4 x y+4 y^2+3 x+6 y-4=0 \\ & (x+2 y)^2+3(x+2 y)-4=0 \\ & (x+2 y+4)(x+2 y-1)=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ The lines are: $$x+2 y+4=0$$ and $$x+2 y-1=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Required distance } & =\frac{|4-(-1)|}{\sqrt{1+4}} \\ & =\frac{5}{\sqrt{5}}=\sqrt{5} \text { units } \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \lambda=\sqrt{5} \text { units } \\ & \lambda^2=5 \end{aligned}$$

content: The distance of a point $$(2,5)$$ from the line $$3 x+y+4=0$$ measured along the line $$L_1$$ and $$L_1$$ are same. If slope of line $$L_1$$ is $$\frac{3}{4}$$, then slope of the line $$\mathrm{L}_2$$ is
explanation:
According to the given condition, in $$\triangle \mathrm{ABC}, \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{AC}$$.

$$\therefore \quad \triangle \mathrm{ABC}$$ is an isosceles triangle.

Let $$\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{m}_1, \mathrm{~m}_2$$ be the slopes of given line, $$\mathrm{L}_1$$ and $$\mathrm{L}_2$$ respectively.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \mathrm{m}=-3, \mathrm{~m}_1=\frac{3}{4} \\ \therefore & \left|\frac{\mathrm{m}-\mathrm{m}_1}{1+\mathrm{mm}_1}\right|=\left|\frac{\mathrm{m}-\mathrm{m}_2}{1+\mathrm{mm}_2}\right| \\ \therefore & 3=\left|\frac{-3-\mathrm{m}_2}{1+3 \mathrm{~m}_2}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}_2=0 \end{array}$$

content: The equations of the lines passing through the point $$(3,2)$$ and making an acute angle of $$45^{\circ}$$ with the line $$x-2 y-3=0$$ are
explanation:
To find the equations of the lines passing through the point (3,2) and making an acute angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the line $x-2y-3=0$, we first need to find the slope of the given line. The slope-intercept form of a line is $y=mx+b$, where $m$ is the slope of the line.

The given line is:

$x-2y-3=0$

Let's rewrite it in slope-intercept form by isolating $y$:

$2y = x - 3$

$y = \frac{1}{2}x - \frac{3}{2}$

Now we have the slope of the given line as $m = \frac{1}{2}$.

Lines that make an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with a given line can be found using the angle between two lines formula. If $m_1$ is the slope of the first line and $m_2$ is the slope of the second line, the tangent of the angle $\theta$ between them is given by:

$\tan(\theta) = \left|\frac{m_2 - m_1}{1 + m_1m_2}\right|$

For our case, $\theta = 45^{\circ}$ and $m_1 = \frac{1}{2}$, so we can plug those values into the formula to find $m_2$, the slope of the lines we're looking for:

$\tan(45^{\circ}) = \left|\frac{m_2 - \frac{1}{2}}{1 + m_2 \cdot \frac{1}{2}}\right|$

$\tan(45^{\circ}) = 1 = \left|\frac{m_2 - \frac{1}{2}}{1 + \frac{m_2}{2}}\right|$

The absolute value gives us two equations to solve, one for each line:

$\frac{m_2 - \frac{1}{2}}{1 + \frac{m_2}{2}} = 1$

$\frac{m_2 - \frac{1}{2}}{1 + \frac{m_2}{2}} = -1$

Let's solve the first equation:

$m_2 - \frac{1}{2} = 1 + \frac{m_2}{2}$

$2m_2 - 1 = 2 + m_2$

$m_2 = 3$

Now the second equation:

$m_2 - \frac{1}{2} = -\left(1 + \frac{m_2}{2}\right)$

$m_2 - \frac{1}{2} = -1 - \frac{m_2}{2}$

$2m_2 - 1 = -2 - m_2$

$3m_2 = -1$

$m_2 = -\frac{1}{3}$

So we have two slopes: $m_2 = 3$ and $m_2 = -\frac{1}{3}$. Now we can use the point-slope form to find the equations of the lines passing through (3,2) with these slopes.

For $m_2 = 3$:

$y - 2 = 3(x - 3)$

$y - 2 = 3x - 9$

$3x - y - 7 = 0$

For $m_2 = -\frac{1}{3}$:

$y - 2 = -\frac{1}{3}(x - 3)$

$y - 2 = -\frac{1}{3}x + 1$

$x + 3y - 9 = 0$

Thus, the equations of the lines are $3x - y - 7 = 0$ and $x + 3y - 9 = 0$, which corresponds to Option B:

$3x - y - 7 = 0$

$x + 3y - 9 = 0$

content: If the polar co-ordinates of a point are $$\left(2, \frac{\pi^{\mathrm{c}}}{4}\right)$$, then its Cartesian co-ordinates are
explanation:
To convert polar coordinates to Cartesian coordinates, we use the following formulas:

$$ x = r \cos(\theta) $$

$$ y = r \sin(\theta) $$

Given the polar coordinates $$\left(2, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$, we have:

$$ r = 2 $$

$$ \theta = \frac{\pi}{4} $$

Now, let's calculate the Cartesian coordinates:

$$ x = 2 \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) $$

$$ y = 2 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) $$

We know that:

$$ \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} $$

$$ \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} $$

So, substituting these values, we get:

$$ x = 2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = \sqrt{2} $$

$$ y = 2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = \sqrt{2} $$

Therefore, the Cartesian coordinates are $$\left(\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2}\right)$$, which corresponds to Option A.

content: The acute angle between the lines $$\left(x^2+y^2\right) \sin \theta+2 x y=0$$ is
explanation:
We have $$\left(x^2+y^2\right) \sin \theta+2 x y=0$$

$$\therefore \mathrm{a}=\sin \theta, \mathrm{b}=\sin \theta$$ and $$\mathrm{h}=1$$ and let $$\alpha$$ be the acute angle between the given lines.

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Then } \tan \alpha=\frac{\mid 2 \sqrt{(1)^2-(\sin \theta)(\sin \theta)}\mid}{\sin \theta+\sin \theta} \\ & =\frac{\left|2 \sqrt{1-\sin ^2 \theta}\right|}{2 \sin \theta}=\frac{2 \cos \theta}{2 \sin \theta}=\cot \theta=\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right) \\ & \therefore \alpha=\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: If the lines represented by $$(k^2+2) x^2+3 x y-6 y^2=0$$ are perpendicular to each other, then the values of $$\mathrm{K}$$ are
explanation:
The lines $$\left(k^2+2\right) x^2+3 x y-6 y^2=0$$ are perpendicular to each other.

$$\therefore\left(k^2+2\right)+(-6)=0 \quad \Rightarrow k^2=4 \Rightarrow k= \pm 2$$

content: The equation of a line with slope $$-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$ and makes an intercept of $$2 \sqrt{2}$$ units on negative direction of $$y$$-axis is
explanation:
The line passes through point $$(0,-2 \sqrt{2})$$ and has slope $$=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}$$.

Hence equation of line is,

$$(y+2 \sqrt{2})=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}(x-0) \Rightarrow x+\sqrt{2} y+4=0$$

content: If the lines respresented by $$a x^2-b x y-y^2=0$$ make angle $$\alpha$$ and $$\beta$$ with the positive direction of $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis, then $$\tan (\alpha+\beta)=$$
explanation:
$$\tan \alpha$$ and $$\tan \beta$$ are roots of the $$a x^2-b x y-y^2=0$$

$$\therefore \tan \alpha+\tan \beta=\frac{-(-b)}{-1}=-b$$ and $$\tan \alpha \tan \beta=\frac{a}{(-1)}=-\mathrm{a}$$

$$\tan (\alpha+\beta)=\frac{\tan \alpha+\tan \beta}{1-\tan \alpha \tan \beta}=\frac{-b}{1-(-a)}=\frac{-b}{1+a}$$

content: If the angle between the lines is $$\frac{\pi^{\mathrm{C}}}{4}$$ and slope of one of the lines is $$\frac{1}{2}$$, then slope of the other line is
explanation:
We have $$\theta=45^{\circ}$$ and $$m_1=\frac{1}{2}$$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \tan \theta=\left|\frac{m_1-m_2}{1+m_1 m_2}\right| \\ & \therefore \tan 45^{\circ}=\left|\frac{\frac{1}{2}-m_2}{1+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) m_2}\right| \Rightarrow\left|\frac{1-2 m_2}{2+m_2}\right|= \pm 1 \\ & \therefore 1-2 m_2=2+m_2 \text { or } 1-2 m_2=-2-m_2 \quad \therefore m_2=\frac{-1}{3} \quad \text { or } m_2=3 \end{aligned}$$

content: If one of the lines given by $$k x^2+x y-y^2=0$$ bisect the angle between the co-ordinate axes then the values of $$k$$ are
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & k^2+x y-y^2=0 \\ & \therefore k+\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2=0 \quad \text{... [Dividing both sides by}~ x^2] \end{aligned}$$

Slope of line is $$\pm 1$$

$$\therefore \mathrm{k}+1-1=0$$ or $$\mathrm{k}-1-1=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=0,2$$

content: The joint equation of pair of lines through the origin and having slopes $$(1+\sqrt{2})$$ and $$\frac{1}{(1+\sqrt{2})}$$ is
explanation:
$$\frac{1}{1+\sqrt{2}}=\frac{(\sqrt{2}-1)}{(\sqrt{2}+1)(\sqrt{2}-1)}=\frac{\sqrt{2}-1}{2-1}=\sqrt{2}-1$$

Hence equations of lines passing through origin and having slopes $$(\sqrt{2}+1)$$ and $$(\sqrt{2}-1)$$ are $$y=(\sqrt{2}+1) x$$ and $$y=(\sqrt{2}-1) x$$.

Their joint equation is $$[(\sqrt{2}+1) x-y][(\sqrt{2}-1) x-y]=0$$

$$\therefore x^2-2 \sqrt{2} x y+y^2=0$$

content: If $$4 a b=3 h^2$$, then the ratio of slopes of the lines represented by $$a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=0$$ is
explanation:
We have $$a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=0$$ and let $$m_1$$ and $$m_2$$ be the slopes of lines.

Now $$\mathrm{m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2=\frac{-2 \mathrm{~h}}{\mathrm{~b}}$$ and $$\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{b}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \left(m_1-m_2\right)^2 & =\left(m_1+m_2\right)^2-4 m_1 m_2 \\ & =\left(\frac{-2 h}{b}\right)^2-4\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)=\frac{4 h^2}{b^2}-\frac{4 a}{b}=\frac{4 h^2-4 a b}{b^2}=\frac{4 h^2-3 h^2}{b^2} \ldots \text { [From data given] } \\ & =\frac{h^2}{b^2} \\ \therefore m_1-m_1 & =\frac{h}{b} \end{aligned}$$

Thus we have $$m_1+m_2=\frac{-2 h}{b}$$ and $$m_1-m_2=\frac{h}{b}$$.

Solving, we get $$m_1=\frac{-h}{2 b}$$ and $$m_2=\frac{-3 h}{2 b} \Rightarrow m_1: m_2=1: 3$$

content: The distance between the lines $$3 x+4 y=9$$ and $$6 x+8 y=15$$ is
explanation:
