Distance between the lines $$=\frac{|18-15|}{\sqrt{(6)^2+(8)^2}}=\frac{3}{10}=0.3$$ units

content: If the slopes of the lines given by the equation $$a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=0$$ are in the ratio $$5: 3$$, then the ratio $$h^2: a b=$$
explanation:
Let $$y=m_1 x$$ and $$y=m_2 x$$ be the lines represented by the equation. $$a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=0$$

Then, $$m_1+m_2=\frac{-2 h}{b}$$ and $$m_1 m_2=\frac{a}{b}$$

We have, $$\frac{m_1}{m_2}=\frac{5}{3} \Rightarrow m_1 \Rightarrow \frac{5 m_2}{3}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \frac{5 m_2}{3}+m_2=\frac{-2 h}{b} \text { and }\left(\frac{5 m_2}{3}\right) m_2=\frac{a}{b} \\ & \therefore \frac{8 m_2}{3}=\frac{-2 h}{b} \Rightarrow m_2=\frac{-3 h}{4 b} \text { and } m_2^2=\frac{3 a}{5 b} \\ & \left(\frac{-3 h}{4 b}\right)^2=\frac{3 a}{5 b} \Rightarrow \frac{9 h^2}{16 b^2}=\frac{3 a}{5 b} \\ & \therefore \frac{h^2}{a b}=\frac{16}{15} \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of line, where length of the perpendicular segment from origin to the line is 4 and the inclination of this perpendicular segment with the positive direction of X-axis is 30$$^\circ$$, is
explanation:
We have $$\ell(\mathrm{OP})=4$$ and $$\mathrm{m} \angle \mathrm{POX}=30^{\circ}$$

$$\therefore \mathrm{P} \equiv\left(4 \cos 30^{\circ}, 4 \sin 30^{\circ}\right) \equiv(2 \sqrt{3}, 2)$$

From figure, we conclude that angle made be line with +ve $$\mathrm{X}$$ axis is $$120^{\circ}$$

$$\therefore$$ Slope of line $$=\tan \left(120^{\circ}\right)=-\sqrt{3}$$

Hence required equation of line L is

$$(y-2)=(-\sqrt{3})(x-2 \sqrt{3}) \Rightarrow \sqrt{3} x+y=8$$

content: If two lines represented by $$a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=0$$ makes angles $$\alpha$$ and $$\beta$$ with positive direction of $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis, then $$\tan (\alpha+\beta)=$$
explanation:
We have $$\tan \alpha+\tan \beta=\frac{-2 h}{b}$$ and $$\tan \alpha \cdot \tan \beta=\frac{a}{b}$$

$$\tan (\alpha+\beta)=\frac{\tan \alpha+\tan \beta}{1-\tan \alpha \tan \beta}$$

$$=\frac{\left(\frac{-2 h}{b}\right)}{1-\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)}=\frac{-2 h}{b} \times \frac{b}{b-a}=\frac{2 h}{a-b}$$

content: The combined equation of a pair of lines passing through the origin and inclined at $$60^{\circ}$$ and $$30=$$ respectively with $$x$$-axis is
explanation:
The equations of two required lines are $$y=\sqrt{3} x$$ and $$y=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} x$$

Their combined equation is $$(\sqrt{3} x-y)(x-\sqrt{3} y)=0$$

i.e. $$\sqrt{3} x^2-4 x y+\sqrt{3} y^2=0$$

content: If the sum of slopes of lines represented by $$\mathrm{ax^2+8xy+5y^2=0}$$ is twice their product, then a =
explanation:
We have $$\mathrm{a x^2+8 x y+5 y^2=0}$$

Here $$\mathrm{m_1+m_2=\frac{-8}{5}, m_1 m_2=\frac{a}{5}}$$

As per condition given, we write $$\left(-\frac{8}{5}\right)=2\left(\frac{a}{5}\right) \Rightarrow a=-4$$

content: If the line joining two points $$\mathrm{A}(2,0)$$ and $$\mathrm{B}(3,1)$$ is rotated about $$\mathrm{A}$$ in anticlockwise direction through an angle of $$15^{\circ}$$, then the equation of the line in new position is
explanation:
Refer figure

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Slope of AB }=\frac{1-0}{3-2}=1 \\ & \therefore \tan \theta=1 \Rightarrow \theta=45^{\circ} \end{aligned}$$

Line AB is rotated through $$15^\circ$$ in anticlockwise direction about A. Therefore in a new position, slope of line $$=\tan \left(45^{\circ}+15^{\circ}\right)=\tan 60^{\circ}=\sqrt{3}$$ and it passes through A.

Required equation of line is $$\begin{aligned} (y-0)=\sqrt{3}(x-2) & \Rightarrow \sqrt{3} x-2 \sqrt{3}=y \end{aligned}$$

content: If lines represented by the equation $$\mathrm{px}^2-\mathrm{qy^{2 }}=0$$ are distinct, then
explanation:
Lines represented by $$\mathrm{px}^2-\mathrm{qy}^2=0$$ are distinct.

Here $$\mathrm{h}=0, \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{p}$$ and $$\mathrm{b}=-\mathrm{q}$$

Now $$h^2-a b>0 \Rightarrow 0+p q>0 \Rightarrow p q>0$$

content: If slope of one of the lines ax$$^2$$ + 2hxy + by$$^2$$ = 0 is twice that of the other, then h$$^2$$ : ab is
explanation:
The given equation represents a pair of straight lines passing through the origin. Let the slopes of these lines be $$m_1$$ and $$m_2$$.

We know that the product of the slopes of the lines represented by the equation $$ax^2 + 2hxy + by^2 = 0$$ is given by: $$m_1m_2 = \frac{a}{b}$$

We are also given that $$m_1 = 2m_2$$. Substituting this into the product of slopes equation, we get:

$$2m_2^2 = \frac{a}{b}$$.

Now, we need to find the relationship between $$h^2$$ and $$ab$$. To do this, we'll use the fact that the sum of the slopes of the lines is given by: $$m_1 + m_2 = -\frac{2h}{b}$$.

Substituting $$m_1 = 2m_2$$, we get: $$3m_2 = -\frac{2h}{b}$$.

Squaring both sides of this equation, we get: $$9m_2^2 = \frac{4h^2}{b^2}$$.

Now, we can substitute the value of $$m_2^2$$ from the product of slopes equation into this equation: $$9 \times \frac{a}{2b} = \frac{4h^2}{b^2}$$.

Simplifying this, we get: $$h^2 = \frac{9ab}{8}$$.

Therefore, $$h^2 : ab = \boxed{9 : 8}$$.

So the correct answer is option C.

content: Area of the triangle formed by the lines $$y^2-9 x y+18 x^2=0$$ and $$y=9$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & y^2-9 x y+18 x^2=0 \\\\ & \therefore(y-3 x)(y-6 x)=0 \end{aligned}$$

Thus three lines forming triangle are

$$y=3 x, y=6 x, y=9$$

Their point of intersections are $$(0,0),(3,9),\left(\frac{3}{2}, 9\right)$$

The area of the triangle formed by the points $$(x_1, y_1)$$, $$(x_2, y_2)$$, and $$(x_3, y_3)$$ is given by:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2) \right| $$

Substituting the points $$(0, 0)$$, $$(3, 9)$$, and $$\left(\frac{3}{2}, 9\right)$$ into the formula:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| 0 (9 - 9) + 3 \left(9 - 0\right) + \frac{3}{2} (0 - 9) \right| $$

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| 0 + 27 - \frac{27}{2} \right| $$

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| \frac{54}{2} - \frac{27}{2} \right| $$

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| \frac{27}{2} \right| $$

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{27}{4} $$

Therefore, the area of the triangle is $$\frac{27}{4}$$ square units. Hence, the correct answer is:

Option C $$\frac{27}{4}$$ sq. units

content: The equation of perpendicular bisector of the line segment joining $$A(-2,3)$$ and $$B(6,-5)$$ is
explanation:
To find the equation of the perpendicular bisector of the line segment joining $ A(-2, 3) $ and $ B(6, -5) $, we need to follow these steps:

Step 1: Determine the midpoint of the line segment $ AB $.

The midpoint $ M $ of a line segment with endpoints $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ is given by the formula:

$$M = \left( \frac{x_1 + x_2}{2}, \frac{y_1 + y_2}{2} \right)$$

Substituting the coordinates of points $ A $ and $ B $:

$$M = \left( \frac{-2 + 6}{2}, \frac{3 - 5}{2} \right)$$

$$M = \left( \frac{4}{2}, \frac{-2}{2} \right)$$

$$M = (2, -1)$$

So, the midpoint $ M $ of line segment $ AB $ is $ (2, -1) $.

Step 2: Find the slope of the line segment $ AB $.

The slope $ m $ of a line passing through points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ is given by the formula:

$$m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$

Substituting the coordinates of points $ A $ and $ B $:

$$m = \frac{-5 - 3}{6 - (-2)}$$

$$m = \frac{-8}{6 + 2}$$

$$m = \frac{-8}{8}$$

$$m = -1$$

So, the slope of line segment $ AB $ is $-1$.

Step 3: Determine the slope of the perpendicular bisector.

The slope of the perpendicular bisector of a line is the negative reciprocal of the slope of the line. Therefore, the slope $ m_{\text{perpendicular}} $ is given by:

$$m_{\text{perpendicular}} = -\frac{1}{m}$$

Since $ m = -1 $:

$$m_{\text{perpendicular}} = -\frac{1}{-1}$$

$$m_{\text{perpendicular}} = 1$$

So, the slope of the perpendicular bisector is $ 1 $.

Step 4: Use the point-slope form to write the equation of the perpendicular bisector.

The point-slope form of the equation of a line with slope $ m $ passing through point $ (x_1, y_1) $ is:

$$y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$$

Using the slope $ m_{\text{perpendicular}} = 1 $ and the midpoint $ (2, -1) $:

$$y - (-1) = 1(x - 2)$$

$$y + 1 = x - 2$$

Rearranging to standard form:

$$x - y = 3$$

Therefore, the equation of the perpendicular bisector is:

Option D: $ x - y = 3 $

content: If $$\mathrm{(m+3 n)(3 m+n)=4 h^2}$$, then the acute angle between the lines represented by $$\mathrm{m x^2+2 h x y+n y^2=0}$$ is
explanation:
We have $$3 m^2+10 m n+3 n^2=4 h^2$$ ..... (1)

Given lines are $$m x^2+2 h x+n y^2=0$$

Let $$m_1$$ and $$m_2$$ be the slopes of the lines

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \mathrm{m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2=\frac{-2 h}{\mathrm{n}} \text { and } \mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}} \\ & \begin{aligned} \text { Now }\left(\mathrm{m}_1-\mathrm{m}_2\right)^2 & =\left(\mathrm{m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2\right)^2-4 \mathrm{~m}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2 \\ & =\left(\frac{-2 h}{\mathrm{n}}\right)^2-\frac{4 \mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{4 \mathrm{~h}^2-4 \mathrm{mn}}{\mathrm{n}^2}=\frac{3 \mathrm{~m}^2+6 \mathrm{mn}+3 \mathrm{n}^2}{\mathrm{n}^2} \quad \ldots[\text { From (1)] } \\ & =\frac{3(\mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{n})^2}{\mathrm{n}^2} \\ \therefore \mathrm{m}_1-\mathrm{m}_2= & \frac{\sqrt{3}(\mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{n})}{\mathrm{n}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Let $$\theta$$ be the required angle.

Now $$\begin{aligned} \tan \theta & =\frac{\left|m_1-m_2\right|}{1+m_1 m_2} \\ & =\frac{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}(m+n)}{n}\right)}{\left(1+\frac{m}{n}\right)}=\frac{\sqrt{3}(m+n)}{n} \times \frac{n}{m+n}=\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow \theta=60^{\circ}=\frac{\pi^c}{3}\end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{p}$$ is the length of the perpendicular from origin to the line whose intercepts on the axes are a and $$b$$, then $$\frac{1}{a^2}+\frac{1}{b^2}=$$
explanation:
Refer figure

$$\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1 \text { i.e. } b x+a y=a b$$ ..... (1)

Distance of line (1) from origin is

$$\begin{array}{ll} \frac{|-a b|}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}=p & \Rightarrow a^2+b^2=\frac{a^2 b^2}{p^2} \\ \therefore \frac{a^2+b^2}{a^2 b^2}=\frac{1}{p^2} & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{a^2}+\frac{1}{b^2}=\frac{1}{p^2} \end{array}$$

content: If the lines $$x^2-4xy+y^2=0$$ make angles $$\alpha$$ and $$\beta$$ with positive direction X-axis, then $$\cot^2\alpha+\cot^2\beta=$$
explanation:
We have lines $$x^2-4 x y+y^2=0$$ and slopes of the lines are $$\tan \alpha$$ and $$\tan \beta$$.

$$\therefore \tan \alpha+\tan \beta=4$$ and $$\tan \alpha \cdot \tan \beta=1$$

$$\therefore \tan \alpha=\frac{1}{\tan \beta}=\cot \beta$$.

$$\therefore \cot \beta+\tan \beta=4$$ and squaring, we get $$\cot ^2 \beta+\tan ^2 \beta+2=16 \Rightarrow \tan ^2 \beta+\cot ^2 \beta=14$$

content: If the two lines given by $$a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=0$$ make inclinations $$\propto$$ and $$\beta$$, then $$\tan (\alpha+\beta)=$$
explanation:
Lines given by $$ax^2+2hxy+by^2=0$$ make inclinations $$\alpha$$ and $$\beta$$.

$$\therefore$$ $$\tan \alpha + \tan \beta = {{ - 2h} \over b}$$ and $$\tan \alpha \tan \beta = {a \over b}$$

Now $$\tan (\alpha + \beta ) = {{\tan \alpha + \tan \beta } \over {1 - \tan \alpha \tan \beta }} = {{\left( { - {{2h} \over b}} \right)} \over {1 - \left( {{a \over b}} \right)}} = {{ - 2h} \over b} \times {b \over {(b - a)}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\tan (\alpha + \beta ) = {{ - 2h} \over {b - a}} = {{2h} \over {a - b}}$$

content: If the polar co-ordinates of a point are $$\left(\sqrt{2}, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$, then its Cartesian co-ordinates are
explanation:
Polar coordinates of $$z=a+i b$$ are $$\left(\sqrt{2}, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \sqrt{2}=\sqrt{a^2+b^2} \Rightarrow a^2+b^2=2 \text { and } \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{b}{a} \Rightarrow \frac{b}{a}=1 \Rightarrow a=b \\ & \therefore a^2+b^2=2 \Rightarrow 2 a^2=2 \Rightarrow a^2=1 \Rightarrow a= \pm 1 \end{aligned}$$

Since point lies in $$1^{\text {st }}$$ quadrant, $$a=1 \Rightarrow b=1$$

$$\therefore$$ Cartesian coordinates are $$(1,1)$$

content: The equation of a line passing through $$(\mathrm{p} \cos \propto, \mathrm{p} \sin \propto)$$ ) and making an angle $$(90+\propto)$$ with positive direction of $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis is
explanation:

content: The product of the perpendicular distances from $$(2,-1)$$ to the pair of lines $$2 x^2-5 x y+2 y^2=0$$ is
explanation:
$$2 x^2-5 x y+2 y^2=0$$

$$\therefore 2 x^2-4 x y-x y+2 y^2=0 \Rightarrow 2 x(x-2 y)-y(x-2 y)=0$$

$$\therefore(2 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y})(\mathrm{x}-2 \mathrm{y})=0 \quad$$ Thus lines are $$2 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}=0$$ and $$\mathrm{x}-2 \mathrm{y}=0$$

Distance of point $$(2,-1)$$ from these two lines are respectively $$\left|\frac{(2)(2)+(-1)(-1)}{\sqrt{4+1}}\right|$$ and $$\left|\frac{(1)(2)+(-1)(-2)}{\sqrt{1+4}}\right|$$ i.e. $$\frac{5}{\sqrt{5}}$$ and $$\frac{4}{\sqrt{5}}$$

Hence required answer is $$\frac{5}{\sqrt{5}} \times \frac{4}{\sqrt{5}}=4$$

content: The x-intercept of a line passing through the points $$\left(\frac{-1}{2}, 1\right)$$ and (1, 2) is :
explanation:
To find the x-intercept of a line, set $y = 0$ and solve for $x$.

First, let's determine the equation of the line passing through the points $\left(\frac{-1}{2}, 1\right)$ and $ (1, 2) $.

Using the point-slope formula, the slope $m$ of the line passing through the points $ (x_1, y_1) $ and $ (x_2, y_2) $ is : $ m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} $

Given $x_1 = \frac{-1}{2}, y_1 = 1, x_2 = 1$, and $y_2 = 2$ :

$ m = \frac{2 - 1}{1 - (-1/2)} $

$ m = \frac{1}{1.5} $

$ m = \frac{2}{3} $

Now, using the point-slope form :

$ y - y_1 = m(x - x_1) $

Substitute the point $\left(\frac{-1}{2}, 1\right)$ and the slope $m = \frac{2}{3}$ into the equation :

$ y - 1 = \frac{2}{3}(x + \frac{1}{2}) $

$ y = \frac{2}{3}x + \frac{1}{3} + 1 $

$ y = \frac{2}{3}x + \frac{4}{3} $

To find the x-intercept, set $y = 0$ :

$ 0 = \frac{2}{3}x + \frac{4}{3} $

$ -\frac{4}{3} = \frac{2}{3}x $

$ x = -2 $

Therefore, the x-intercept of the line is $-2$. The correct answer is Option B.

content: If the equation $$3x^2-kxy-3y^2=0$$ represents the bisectors of angles between the lines $$x^2-3xy-4y^2=0$$, then value of k is
explanation:
We have $$x^2-3 x y-4 y^2=0$$ and comparing it with standard equation, we write $$\mathrm{A}=1, \mathrm{H}=\frac{-3}{2}, \mathrm{~B}=-4$$.

Equation of bisector of angle of this line is

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x^2-y^2}{A-B}=\frac{x y}{H} \Rightarrow \frac{x^2-y^2}{1+4}=\frac{x y}{\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)} \\ & \therefore-3 x^2+3 y^2=10 x y \Rightarrow 3 x^2+10 x y-3 y^2=0 \\ & \text { Comparing with given equation, we get } k=-10 \end{aligned}$$

Comparing with given equation, we get $$\mathrm{k}=-10$$

content: The joint equation of pair of lines through the origin and making an equilateral triangle with the line $$y=3$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\triangle \mathrm{OAB}$$ be the required triangle. Since $$\triangle \mathrm{OAB}$$ is an equilateral triangle.

Slope of line $$\mathrm{OA}=\tan 60^{\circ}=\sqrt{3}$$ and Slope of line $$\mathrm{OB}=\tan 120^{\circ}=-\sqrt{3}$$

$$\therefore$$ Equation of $$\mathrm{OA}$$ is $$y=\sqrt{3} x$$ i.e. $$\sqrt{3} x-y=0$$ and equation of OB is $$y=-\sqrt{3} x$$ i.e. $$\sqrt{3} x+y=0$$

Hence required joint equation is

$$(\sqrt{3} x-y)(\sqrt{3} x+y)=0 \text { i.e. } 3 x^2-y^2=0$$

content: If the acute angle between the lines given by $$\mathrm{a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=0}$$ is $$\frac{\pi}{4}$$, then $$\mathrm{4 h^2=}$$

content: The joint equation of the pair of lines through the origin and making an equilateral triangle with the line $$x=3$$ is

content: The slope of the line through the origin which makes an angle of 30$$^\circ$$ with the positive direction of Y-axis measured anticlockwise is :

content: If the slopes of the lines given by the equation $a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=0$ are in the ratio $5: 3$, then the ratio $h^2: a b=$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given line, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=0 \\ & \therefore \quad m_1+m_2=-\frac{2 h}{b} \\ & \qquad m_1 m_2=\frac{a}{b} \\ & \because \quad\left(m_1+m_2\right)^2-\left(m_1-m_2\right)^2=4 m_1 m_2 \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{4 h^2}{b^2}-\left(m_1-m_2\right)^2=\frac{4 a}{b} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{4 h^2}{b^2}-\left(m_1-m_2\right)^2=\frac{4 a}{b} \\ & \Rightarrow\qquad m_1-m_2=\frac{2 \sqrt{h^2-a b}}{b} \\ & \text { Given, } \quad \frac{m_1}{m_2}=\frac{5}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{m_1+m_2}{m_1-m_2}=\frac{5+3}{5-3} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{-2 h / b}{\frac{2 \sqrt{h^2-a b}}{b}}=4 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad-h=4 \sqrt{h^2-a b} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad h^2=16\left(h^2-a b\right) \\ & \qquad 15 h^2=16 a b \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{h^2}{a b}=\frac{16}{15} \end{aligned}$$

content: The equation of a line passing through the point $(7,-4)$ and perpendicular to the line passing through the points $(2,3)$ and $(1,-2)$ is

content: If the equation $3 x^2+10 x y+3 y^2+16 y+k=0$ represents a pair of lines, then the value of kis

content: If the equation $$a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2+2 g x+2 f y=0$$ has one line as the bisector of the angle between co-ordinate axes, then
explanation:
Given, equation of line

$$a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2+2 g x+2 f y=0$$

Let the slope of line is $$m_1$$ and $$m_2$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad m_1+m_2 & =\frac{-2 h}{b} \\ m_1 m_2 & =\frac{a}{b} \end{aligned}$$

Given, one line is bisector of angle between coordinate axes.

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} & \therefore \quad m_1 =\tan \frac{\pi}{4}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 1+m_2 =-2 h / b \quad \text{... (i)}\\ & \qquad m_2 =\frac{a}{b} \quad \text{... (ii)} \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get }\\ \begin{aligned} &\quad 1+\frac{a}{b} =\frac{-2 h}{b} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a+b =-2 h \\ & \quad (a+b)^2 =4 h^2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the equation $$k x y+5 x+3 y+2=0$$ represents a pair of lines, then $$k=$$
explanation:
Given equation of pairs of lines

$$k x y+5 x+3 y+2=0$$

We know that, if

$$a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2+2 g x+2 f y+c=0$$

is a pair of straight line, then

$$\begin{aligned} & a b c+2 f g h-a f^2-b g^2-c h^2=0 \\ & \text { in equation, } k x y+5 x+3 y+2=0 \\ & \text { Here, } a=0, b=0, h=k / 2, g=5 / 2, f=\frac{3}{2}, c=2 \end{aligned}$$

Here, $$a=0, b=0, h=k / 2, g=5 / 2, f=\frac{3}{2}, c=2$$

$$\begin{gathered} \therefore 0+2\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)\left(\frac{k}{2}\right)-0-0-2\left(\frac{k}{2}\right)^2=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{15 k}{4}-\frac{k^2}{2}=0 \Rightarrow \frac{k}{2}\left(\frac{15}{2}-k\right)=0 \\ =k=\frac{15}{2} k \neq 0 \text {, if } k=0 \end{gathered}$$

Then only one straight line.

content: If $$(a,-2 a), a>0$$ is the mid-point of a line segment intercepted between the co-ordinate axes, then the equation of the line is

Now, $$(a,-2 a)$$ is mid-point of intercept

$$\therefore a=\frac{A}{2}$$ and $$-2 a=B / 2 \Rightarrow A=2 a$$ and $$B=-4 a$$

$$\therefore$$ Required equation of line is

$$\frac{x}{2 a}-\frac{y}{4 a}=1 \Rightarrow 2 x-y=4 a$$

content: If the angle between the lines given by the equation $$x^2-3 x y+\lambda y^2+3 x-5 y+2=0, \lambda \geq 0$$, is $$\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)$$, then $$\lambda=$$
explanation:
Given, line

$$x^2-3 x y+\lambda y^2+3 x-5 y+2=0$$

Here, $$a=1, h=-3 / 2, b=\lambda$$

Angle between pairs of line is

$$\begin{aligned} \tan \theta & =\frac{2 \sqrt{h^2-a b}}{a+b} \\ \tan \left(\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{3}\right) & =\frac{2 \sqrt{\frac{9}{4}-\lambda}}{1+\lambda} \end{aligned}$$

$$\frac{1}{3}=\frac{2 \sqrt{\frac{9}{4}-\lambda}}{\lambda+1} \Rightarrow \lambda+1=6 \sqrt{\frac{9-4 \lambda}{4}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad(\lambda+1)^2=\frac{36}{4}(9-4 \lambda) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \lambda^2+2 \lambda+1=81-36 \lambda \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda^2+38 \lambda-80=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\lambda+40)(\lambda-2)=0 \\ & \therefore \quad \lambda=2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The joint equation of lines passing through origin and having slopes $(1+\sqrt{2})$ and $\frac{-1}{1+\sqrt{2}}$ is ..........
explanation:
It is given that slopes of the lines passing through origin are

$$\begin{aligned} m_1(\text { let })=(1+\sqrt{2}) \text { and } m_2(\text { let }) & =\frac{-1}{1+\sqrt{2}} \\ & =-(\sqrt{2}-1) \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore$ Required joint equation of lines passing through origin is

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { origin is } \\ & {[y-(1+\sqrt{2}) x][y+(\sqrt{2}-1) x]=0} \\ & \Rightarrow y^2+(\sqrt{2}-1) x y-(1+\sqrt{2}) x y-(2-1) x^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y^2-2 x y-x^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+2 x y-y^2=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: If sum of the slopes of the lines given by $x^2-4 p x y+8 y^2=0$ is three times their product then $p=$ ...........

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} \Rightarrow & & m_1+m_2 & =3 \times\left(m_1 m_2\right) \\ \Rightarrow & & \frac{-(-4 p)}{8} & =3 \times \frac{1}{8} \\ \Rightarrow & & 4 p=3 \Rightarrow p=\frac{3}{4} \end{array}$$

content: If lines represented by $$\left(1+\sin ^2 \theta\right) x^2+2 h x y+2 \sin \theta y^2=0, \theta \in[0,2 \pi]$$ are perpendicular to each other then $\theta=$ ...........
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { we have, pair of lines represented by }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \left(1+\sin ^2 \theta\right) x^2+2 h x y+2 \sin \theta y^2=0, \theta \in[0,2 \pi] \\ &\therefore\quad \left(1+\sin ^2 \theta\right)+2 \sin \theta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(1+\sin \theta)^2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 1+\sin \theta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta=-1=-\sin \frac{\pi}{2}=\sin \left(2 \pi-\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\sin \frac{3 \pi}{2} \Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{3 \pi}{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $(-\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2})$ are cartesian co-ordinates of the point, then its polar co-ordinates are .........
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text {We have, } r \cos \theta=-\sqrt{2} \text { and } r \sin \theta=\sqrt{2} \\ & \therefore r^2 \cos ^2 \theta+r^2 \sin ^2 \theta=2+2=4 \\ & \Rightarrow r^2\left(\cos ^2 \theta+\sin ^2 \theta\right)=4 \\ & \Rightarrow r= \pm 2 \text { and } \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{-\sqrt{2}}\right) \\ & \theta=\tan ^{-1}(-1) \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{3 \pi}{4} \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \text { Required polar co-ordinate is }(r, \theta)\\ &=\left(2, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right) \end{aligned}$$

content: The $y$-intercept of the line passing through $A(6,1)$ and perpendicular to the line $x-2 y=4$ is ...........
explanation:
Slope of the given line,

$$x-2 y=4 \text { is } \frac{1}{2}$$

Equation of a line passing through $A(6,1)$ and perpendicular to given line is

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} & & y-1=(-2)(x-6) \\ \Rightarrow & & y-1 =-2 x+12 \\ \Rightarrow & & 2 x+y=13 \\ \Rightarrow & & \frac{x}{\left(\frac{13}{2}\right)}+\frac{y}{13} =1\quad\text{... (i)} \end{array}$$

$\therefore y$-intercept of slope (i) is 13.

content: The joint equation of pair of straight lines passing through origin and having slopes $(1+\sqrt{2})$ and $\left(\frac{1}{1+\sqrt{2}}\right)$ is .......
explanation:
Required equation of pair of straight lines passing through origin and having slopes,

$$\begin{aligned} & m_1=(1+\sqrt{2}) \text { and } m_2=\frac{1}{1+\sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{2}-1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad& {[y-(1+\sqrt{2}) x][y-(\sqrt{2}-1) x]=0 } \\ \Rightarrow \quad& y^2-(\sqrt{2}-1) x y-(1+\sqrt{2}) x y+(2-1) x^2=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad& y^2-(\sqrt{2}-1+1+\sqrt{2}) x y+x^2=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad& y^2-2 \sqrt{2} x y+x^2=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: The joint equation of the lines passing through the origin and trisecting the first quadrant is
explanation:
Since, lines passing through the origin and trisecting the first quadrant therefore there slopes are

$$\begin{array}{ll} & m_1=\tan 30^{\circ}, m_2=\tan 60^{\circ} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & m_1=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, m_2=\sqrt{3} \end{array}$$

$\therefore$ Required joint equation of the lines is

$$\begin{array}{rrr} & \left(y-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} x\right)(y-\sqrt{3} x) & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & (\sqrt{3} y-x)(y-\sqrt{3} x) & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & \sqrt{3} y^2-3 x y-x y+\sqrt{3} x^2 & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & \sqrt{3} x^2-4 x y+\sqrt{3} y^2 & =0 \end{array}$$

content: If $P(2,2), Q(-2,4)$ and $R(3,4)$ are the vertices of $\triangle P Q R$ then the equation of the median through vertex $R$ is ......
explanation:
Mid-point of point $P(2,2)$ and $Q(-2,4)$ is

$$\left(\frac{2-2}{2}, \frac{2+4}{2}\right)$$

i.e. $(0,3)$

Required equation of median passing through vertex $R$ is

$$\begin{array}{cc} & y-4=\frac{3-4}{0-3}(x-3) \\ \Rightarrow & y-4=\frac{1}{3}(x-3) \\ \Rightarrow & 3 y-12=x-3 \\ \Rightarrow & x-3 y+9=0 \end{array}$$
